n: Yamal anthrax burial grounds
host: W006 (TBD — cartographer to confirm from geometry)
det: Yamal Peninsula, Siberia · 2016 outbreak (permafrost thaw) — Not cursed earth — cursed graves. Seven thousand of them, dug shallow because permafrost does not dig, holding animals that died of anthrax when a million reindeer died. The soil itself tests clean and is too acid to hold a spore. It is what was put in it that keeps its word.

n: Djibouti City — Anopheles stephensi bridgehead
host: TBD — cartographer to assign
ctx: Djibouti City, Horn of Africa · from 2012
det: Djibouti City, Horn of Africa · from 2012 — A mosquito got off a boat in 2012 and made malaria an urban disease. It breeds in the water barrel, not the marsh, and it works through the dry season when the old vectors cannot. In seven years the city's rate went up roughly forty-fold. It is in eight countries now.

n: Dire Dawa — the resistant convergence
host: R132 (TBD — Dire Dawa sits low on the highland margin; confirm)
det: Dire Dawa, Ethiopia · 2023 (urban malaria) — The mosquito that likes cities met the parasite the test cannot see. Both were rare. Here they are the same outbreak, in the dry season, when malaria is not supposed to happen.

n: Doi Inthanon — 140 species in one park
ctx: Doi Inthanon NP, northern Thailand · present
det: Doi Inthanon NP, northern Thailand · present — One mountain park, one hundred and forty kinds of mosquito — a twenty-fifth of every species there is. Most of them want nothing to do with us. The richness sits in the forest, not the water jars, which is the opposite of how the dangerous ones live.

n: Panamanian isthmus — the density peak
host: TBD — cartographer to assign (R032 covers Guatemala-Costa Rica only)
det: Panamanian isthmus · present — Brazil has more kinds of mosquito than anywhere. Brazil is also enormous. Correct for that and the crown goes to the isthmus — the narrow place the continents pass through, which is where most things end up densest.

n: Ouidah — the two trees
host: R-zone: Benin coast (Bight of Benin) — host zone to be assigned by research
det: Ouidah, Benin · slave-trade era, 18th–19th c. — Two trees, three kilometres apart, on the road to the water. At the first one the men walked round nine times and the women seven — the numbers that mean man and woman here — so that they would forget where they came from and never find their way back. At the second the kings planted an anchor for the ghosts, because they were afraid that the ones who died at sea would return for revenge. One ritual to erase the person. One to park the spirit somewhere it could not reach them. Both administered by the people selling them. And the Tree of Forgetting is gone — nobody knows exactly where it stood; there is a statue at the approximate spot and a sapling planted in the nineties. The tree that took the memory has been forgotten.

n: Ibini Ukpabi — the oracle that ate people
host: R-zone: southeastern Nigeria (Cross River / Abia) — host zone to be assigned by research
det: Arochukwu, Nigeria · oracle to 1902 — You brought your dispute to the oracle — murder, witchcraft, poisoning, a family quarrel. Its judgement was final and no wealth on earth could reverse it. If it found against you, you walked into the tunnel; at the Hill of Rags you took off your clothes and left them there; and you were never seen again. Then the river ran red, so your family, waiting outside, knew the god had eaten you. There was no blood. You were walked out the back and sold, and your clothes stayed on the hill. It needed no army and no raiders. The supply came voluntarily, brought by its own neighbours, looking for justice.

n: Lower Senegal River — the dam that bred the fluke
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign
ctx: Lower Senegal River, Senegal/Mauritania · from 1986 (Diama Dam)
det: Lower Senegal River, Senegal/Mauritania · from 1986 (Diama Dam) — They dammed the river mouth to keep the sea out so the water would stay sweet for the rice. Eighteen months later a disease that had never been here was in the water — the snail that carries it likes sweet still water, and the prawn that eats the snail could no longer get upriver past the dam. In one town the infected went from two in a hundred to seven in ten in a single year.

n: The river-blindness valleys
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Volta basin — multi-country pattern, not a point)
ctx: Volta basin, West Africa · 20th c.; control from 1974
det: Volta basin, West Africa · 20th c.; control from 1974 — The good soil is by the river, and the river is where the blackfly breeds, and the fly's bite goes slowly blind. For generations the fertile valleys of the north sat empty and the colonial officers could not say why; the people who had left could. Half the old men were blind. The land is good and the flies keep it empty.

n: The Nipah belt
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (central/NW Bangladesh; Faridpur epicentre)
det: central/NW Bangladesh (Faridpur) · from 2001 — Every winter people tap the date palms for sweet sap and drink it fresh overnight. The fruit bats drink from the same cuts, and their spit carries a virus that kills two people in three. It passes person to person as well. It keeps to a belt of districts in the middle of the country, worst around one town, and it is starting to come off the sap and onto the fruit the bats have bitten.

n: Kyasanur forest — the tick in the trees
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Western Ghats; INFORMATIONAL if on a settled R-zone — no population effect, per K004)
det: Western Ghats, Karnataka, India · since 1957 — The forest gives warning: the monkeys die first. Then the tick that fed on them finds a person, and the fever it carries bleeds you from the inside, and there is no cure for it. It began in one Karnataka forest in 1957 and has walked into five states since, worst where the woods have been cut into pieces. The edge of the trees is the dangerous place — not the deep forest, and not the town.

n: Runit Dome, Enewetak — the Tomb the sea opens
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Enewetak Atoll, Marshall Islands)
ctx: Enewetak Atoll, Marshall Islands · tests 1946–58; dome 1977
det: Enewetak Atoll, Marshall Islands · tests 1946–58; dome 1977 — They tested sixty-seven bombs on two atolls and moved the people who lived there off their islands. Then they scooped the poisoned dirt into a crater one of the bombs had made, poured a lid of concrete over it, and called it done. The locals call it the Tomb. It has no floor — the sea already runs through the coral underneath it — and the plutonium inside stays deadly longer than there has been writing. The island is two metres above the water. The lid is cracking.

n: Poveglia — the island that keeps its dead
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Venetian lagoon)
ctx: Poveglia, Venetian Lagoon, Italy · plague island; asylum to 1968
det: Poveglia, Venetian Lagoon, Italy · plague island; asylum to 1968 — A small island in the lagoon where Venice put the things it wanted gone. First the plague — thousands quarantined and buried, so many that the ground is part ash and the bone still washes up, and the fishermen will not cast near it. Then, for forty years, the people it called mad. The stories of the doctor who threw himself off the tower are probably invented; the graves are not. You cannot land there now — not for fear of the dead, but because the roofs are coming down.

n: The killer lakes of Cameroon
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (NW Cameroon; Nyos & Monoun)
det: Lakes Nyos & Monoun, NW Cameroon · 1984 & 1986 — The people around the lake always said it could kill, and in 1986 it did: a crater lake breathed out a cloud of carbon dioxide in the night, heavier than air, and it rolled down the valleys and smothered seventeen hundred people where they slept. A smaller lake nearby had done it two years before. The gas seeps up from the rock and gathers in the deep cold water until something tips it loose. It has done this for as long as the rock has been there, and it will do it again.

n: Ilha da Queimada Grande — Snake Island
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (off Sao Paulo, Brazil). DEDUP FLAG with Species Refugia
ctx: Ilha da Queimada Grande, off São Paulo, Brazil · present
det: Ilha da Queimada Grande, off São Paulo, Brazil · present — An island the sea made eleven thousand years ago, when it rose and cut a strip of coast loose with a few snakes on it. With nothing to hunt but birds and nothing hunting them, the snakes multiplied and their venom turned quick and terrible. There are a few thousand now and they live nowhere else on earth. The navy has kept people off since the 1920s — half to save the visitor, half to save the snake from the people who would sell it.

n: Hierapolis Ploutonion — the Gate to Hell
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Pamukkale, SW Turkey)
ctx: Hierapolis, Pamukkale, SW Turkey · Greco-Roman; excavated 2013
det: Hierapolis, Pamukkale, SW Turkey · Greco-Roman; excavated 2013 — They called it the gate to hell, and they were not wrong about what it did. A crack in the rock breathes out carbon dioxide, heavier than air, so it lies in an invisible sheet along the ground; birds that fly through it fall, and the beasts the priests led in died at once, which everyone took as the god accepting them. The priests walked in and out alive because they were tall enough to keep their heads above the gas and knew the hours it thinned. The fear was true. It kept people out of a place that kills you for standing in the wrong air.

n: The tsunami stones of Sanriku
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Sanriku coast, Iwate; Aneyoshi/Miyako representative)
ctx: Sanriku coast, Iwate, Japan · stones after 1896 & 1933; borne out 2011
det: Sanriku coast, Iwate, Japan · stones after 1896 & 1933; borne out 2011 — All along this coast there are stones, some six hundred years old, and they say one thing: do not build below this point. People who lived through the great waves cut the warning where the water stopped, because they knew memory only lasts three generations. Where a village kept faith with its stone and built above it, the village lived; in 2011 the sea climbed to within a hundred metres of the Aneyoshi stone and no further. Where towns trusted new seawalls instead and built low, the sea took them. The old line was the true one.

n: The tsetse country — the green desert
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (sub-Saharan belt — multi-country, diffuse; cf. K009)
ctx: sub-Saharan tsetse belt · endemic; colonial era
det: sub-Saharan tsetse belt · endemic; colonial era — There is good land across the middle of Africa — moist valleys, deep grass — that people left empty on purpose, because a fly lives there whose bite brings a sleep you do not wake from, and a wasting death for cattle. They worked out the fly was the cause a long time ago and simply kept away, siting their villages and herds clear of its country. So the fly became, without meaning to, the keeper of the wild: the places it holds are the places the herds and the ploughs never reached. The fear does real work. It has guarded the grass for a very long time.

n: The Pontine Marshes — mal'aria
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Agro Pontino, Lazio, S of Rome)
ctx: Agro Pontino, Lazio, Italy · malarial to the 1930s
det: Agro Pontino, Lazio, Italy · malarial to the 1930s — For two thousand years the marsh south of Rome killed anyone who tried to live in it, and the people blamed the air — mal'aria, bad air — and built their houses with no windows facing the swamp. They had the cause wrong and the danger right: it was the mosquito, not the smell, but staying out of the bad-air country was the correct thing to do. Emperors and popes tried to drain it and failed. It stayed empty and fatal until the twentieth century finally pumped it dry.

n: Spinalonga & the leper islands
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Spinalonga, Crete; Kalaupapa, Molokai as analog)
det: Spinalonga, Crete, Greece · leper colony 1903–1957 — An island where you were sent to disappear. If the disease showed on you, they crossed your name out of the town book as if you had died, put you on a boat, and left you on the rock; the patients called the gate they came through Dante's Gate, after the door to hell. It served as a wall between the sick and the well for half a century. But leprosy barely spreads and has been curable since the 1940s, so the wall was built mostly of fear — the island did its job, and its job was mostly cruelty.

n: Corryvreckan — the Hag
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (between Jura and Scarba, Argyll, Scotland)
ctx: off Jura, Argyll, Scotland · tidal whirlpool
det: off Jura, Argyll, Scotland · tidal whirlpool — The rock in the middle of the strait is called the Hag, and the sea around it is the third-biggest whirlpool on earth. When the tide pours through and strikes the pinnacle the water stands up in walls and roars for ten miles, and the old story is that it is the winter hag washing her great plaid white. The navy wrote it down as unnavigable. The face they gave the danger is exactly where the danger is: a hag-shaped rock that will drown you.

n: Mefite — the valley of the goddess Mefitis
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Rocca San Felice, Irpinia, Campania, Italy)
ctx: Rocca San Felice, Campania, Italy · volcanic gas; ancient shrine
det: Rocca San Felice, Campania, Italy · volcanic gas; ancient shrine — A little crater in the Italian hills where the ground boils and breathes out a gas that still drops birds out of the air. The people who lived here did not call it a gas; they called it a goddess, Mefitis, and built her a temple, and Virgil wrote that this was one of the doors to the underworld. We kept her name without knowing it: mephitic, the word for air you cannot breathe. The danger was given a face and a shrine, and the face was correct — you still do not stand where she breathes.

n: Taniwha — the water that does not behave
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Aotearoa/NZ; Meremere/Waikato representative — diffuse class)
det: Aotearoa / New Zealand · Māori tradition — The Maori word for the places where water turns dangerous is a living thing: a taniwha, a being that holds a stretch of river or coast where the current drags and the breakers come from the wrong direction. Some are guardians and some are killers, but either way the rule is the same — that pool, that channel, that bend is not to be crossed lightly. In 2002 a road was moved away from one taniwha's ground over official protest; a year later the river flooded exactly there. The monster was a map, and the map was right.

n: Namazu — the catfish under the country
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Kashima Jingu, Ibaraki, Japan — the pinning-stone; hazard is nationwide)
det: Japan (Kashima Jingū) · folklore; 1855 catfish prints — Under Japan there is a catfish the size of the country, and when it moves the ground moves; that is the old account of an earthquake. A thunder-god pins its head with a stone at Kashima and its tail with a stone at Katori, and only a hand's width of each stone shows above the earth — the rest, they say, goes down forever. When the god is away the fish gets loose and the land shakes. They named the most relentless danger in the islands and gave it a keeper. The danger is entirely real; the keeper is a hope.

n: The Grootslang's pit — the Wonder Hole
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Richtersveld, Northern Cape, South Africa — Orange River/Namibia border; exact Wonder Hole location vague)
ctx: Richtersveld, Northern Cape, South Africa · folklore
det: Richtersveld, Northern Cape, South Africa · folklore — In the desert where South Africa meets the Orange River there is a hole no one has found the bottom of, and the story is that a thing lives in it — part elephant, part serpent, older than either — guarding a hoard of diamonds and eating the men who come for them. A prospector really did walk toward that hole in 1917 and never came back. The pit is real and its depth is unknown, and the beast is the shape the danger takes: go down after the diamonds and the ground keeps you.

n: The kelpie's loch — each-uisge
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Scottish Highlands — diffuse; nearly every loch)
det: Scottish Highlands · folk tradition — A fine horse stands grazing by the loch with its mane wet and tangled, and if you climb on, your skin sticks fast to its hide and it walks you calmly into the deep water and drowns you. Nearly every loch in Scotland has one. The folklorists are blunt about what it was for: the fast rivers and sudden drop-offs and weed-hidden edges kill people, and a horse-shaped monster is a warning a child remembers when a fence would not. Do not climb on the strange horse by the water.

n: Peg Powler's Tees — the green-teeth hags
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (River Tees — Peg Powler; NW England ponds — Jenny Greenteeth; diffuse)
ctx: River Tees & NW England · folk tradition
det: River Tees & NW England · folk tradition — The green mat on the still pond looks like grass you could run across, and the name for it is also the name of the thing that lives beneath it: Jenny Greenteeth, or on the River Tees, Peg Powler — a green hag with weed for hair who takes hold of children who lean too close and pulls them under. The monster and the danger are one word. Parents made her up, and they made her up for a true reason: the weed-covered water is exactly the water that drowns a child who trusts the surface.

n: The Wendigo's winter — the boreal famine
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (subarctic boreal forest, N Canada / N US — diffuse)
ctx: subarctic boreal forest, Canada/US · Algonquian tradition
det: subarctic boreal forest, Canada/US · Algonquian tradition — In the long winters of the northern forest, where the game thins and a lone person can starve, the danger has a name and a shape: the Wendigo, a gaunt giant made of hunger, that grows as it eats and is never full, and that can get inside a person driven to eat other people. It is a rule dressed as a monster — do not hoard while others starve, do not wander off alone into the cold, do not cross the one line that turns a starving person into something that is no longer a person. The winter really does kill that way. The story is how you teach a child to survive it.

n: Scylla & Charybdis — the Strait of Messina
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Strait of Messina, Sicily/Calabria)
ctx: Strait of Messina, Italy · Greek myth (1908 quake)
det: Strait of Messina, Italy · Greek myth (1908 quake) — Two seas meet in a three-kilometre gap and the water cannot decide which way to go, so it turns several times a day and spins into whirlpools and stands up in sudden walls. Homer gave the two sides two monsters: a six-headed thing on the rocks of the Calabrian shore, and a whirlpool on the Sicilian one that swallowed ships whole. To be caught between them became the phrase for being trapped between two dangers. There were no monsters; there was a strait that killed oared ships, and two names to remember which side did it how.

n: The will-o'-the-wisp — the Lantern Men
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (English Fens & boglands; cross-cultural — diffuse)
det: English Fens & boglands · folk tradition — A little light bobs over the bog at night, the size of a candle-flame, and if you follow it — thinking it a lantern, a house, a person ahead — it walks you off the dry path and out onto the reed-bed and the open mire, and then it goes out, and the ground stops holding you. The light is real; it is marsh gas catching in the dark. What the story got right long before the chemists did is the only thing that matters out there: a light over a bog at night is a thing to turn your back on.

n: Yuki-onna — the snow country
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (snow country — Tohoku/Echigo, Japan; diffuse)
det: Tōhoku/Echigo, Japan · folklore; recorded 1904 — On the worst nights of the snow country a woman comes with the blizzard, tall and white, and leaves no tracks. She breathes on the traveler who is already lost and he does not feel cold any more; or she walks a little ahead and he follows her further from the road until the snow has him. What she is, is exposure — the way a person freezing to death stops shivering, feels warm, and lies down. The story keeps the true rule close: do not travel into the storm, and if you are caught, reach the fire. Warmth is the only thing she cannot stand.

n: Ijiraq & Amarok — the open Arctic
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Arctic tundra, Inuit Nunangat — Baffin representative; diffuse)
det: Arctic, Inuit Nunangat · Inuit tradition — On the open ice two dangers wait, and the Inuit gave each a shape. One is the Ijiraq, which hides the path and turns you around until the white has no direction left in it and you do not come back; against it people raised stone figures, the inuksuit, to hold the way. The other is the Amarok, a wolf the size of a nightmare that takes the person who went out alone — because to travel the tundra by yourself is the mistake that kills, and the wolf is that mistake made flesh. Keep the landmarks. Do not go alone.

n: The upas tree — the death tree of Java
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Java, Indonesia; the tree is widespread across the Old-World tropics)
det: Java, Indonesia · myth; debunked 1783–1810 — They said there was a tree in Java so poisonous that nothing grew for miles around it, that the ground beneath was white with the bones of birds and men, and that only condemned criminals were sent to gather its sap, few of them coming back. Almost none of that is true — it was a traveller's hoax, printed in 1783 and repeated by poets until everyone believed it. What is true is smaller and real: the tree's sap is a genuine poison, the kind the hunters here have tipped their darts with for centuries. A drop will stop a dog's heart in minutes. The death-valley was invented. The poison was not.

n: The Leshy — the deep forest
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Eastern European forests — Polissia representative; diffuse)
det: Eastern European forests · Slavic folklore — Walk far enough into the old forest and the path begins to twist: you go straight for an hour and come back to the clearing you left, and your certainty about which way is out quietly falls apart. The people of these woods had a name for that feeling — the Leshy, the old man of the trees, is leading you. He mimics a voice you know and you follow it the wrong way. What he is, is the plain fact that a vast forest erases direction, and a person who trusts their sense of it will circle until dark. Leave a gift at the treeline. Do not stray from the path.

n: Lady Midday — Poludnica
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Slavic agricultural fields — diffuse, E. Europe)
det: Slavic fields, Eastern Europe · folklore — At noon in high summer, when the reapers should be resting, a woman in white walks the edges of the fields, and the one who works on through the worst heat feels her hand on the back of the neck: the stagger, the pounding head, the sudden madness of sunstroke. The old people did not call it sunstroke; they called it Lady Midday and stopped work when she was abroad. The rule she carried is a true one. Do not labour in the field at the top of the day. Rest in the shade until she has passed.

n: The Nuckelavee
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Orkney Islands, Scotland — Stronsay)
det: Orkney, Scotland · folklore; recorded 1890s — When the grain blackened in the field and the horses sickened and died and the rain would not come, the people of Orkney knew who to blame: a thing from the sea with no skin, its black blood showing through, that breathed a poison over the land as it walked the shore at night. What it was, was every disaster they could not explain — the blight, the murrain, the drought — given one body to hate. And because they gave it a body, they gave it rules: it cannot cross running fresh water, and it will not come out in the rain. A person could survive the thing they feared most by keeping a stream between themselves and it.

n: The Moskstraumen — the Maelstrom
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Lofoten, Nordland, Norway)
det: Lofoten, Norway · maelstrom; 1539 & 1841 accounts — Off the Lofoten islands the tide pours over a shallow ridge into deep water and the sea turns — not a single funnel but a field of whirlpools and racing eddies, one of the strongest currents in the world. This is the first maelstrom, the one that gave the word to every language; a mapmaker in 1539 wrote 'Horrenda Caribdis' across it, naming it the northern twin of Homer's whirlpool. Poe and Verne made it a mile-wide throat that swallowed ships and whales, which it is not. What it is, is enough: a real current that will take a small boat and not give it back.

n: The Qalupalik
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Arctic coasts — Inuit Nunangat, Greenland/Canada/Alaska; diffuse)
ctx: Arctic coasts, Inuit Nunangat · Inuit tradition
det: Arctic coasts, Inuit Nunangat · Inuit tradition — Under the sea-ice something hums, low and sweet, and taps on the underside of the ice where a child is playing, and the child leans closer to hear — and a green hand comes up through the crack and takes them down into the cold, into the pouch on its back, and they are not seen again. What it is, is the ice that will not hold and the water that kills in a minute. The parents gave that danger a face and a song so their children would learn the one rule that keeps them alive up here: do not play at the edge of the ice.

n: La Llorona — the weeping women of the rivers
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Latin America & US Southwest — La Llorona; Slavic rivers — Rusalka; diffuse)
ctx: Latin America & US Southwest · folklore; c. 1550
det: Latin America & US Southwest · folklore; c. 1550 — By the river at night you may hear a woman weeping for her children, and if you follow the sound to comfort her she will take you into the water, because in her grief she drowns the children she finds. Every river country has a version of her — the weeping woman here, the drowned girl of the Slavic lakes, and others. What she is, under the ghost, is the plain danger of the dark river to a child who wanders to its edge after dark. Mothers gave the water a grieving voice so their children would come home. Stay back from the bank at night.

n: The simoom — the poison wind
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Sahara & Arabian deserts — diffuse)
det: Sahara & Arabian deserts · desert wind — A wind comes off the desert that the people who live there call poison — samum — because it kills. It is brief, twenty minutes maybe, but it is hotter than blood and dry as ash and carries the sand in a spinning wall, and it brings a body more heat than sweating can lose, so a person caught in it can drop of heatstroke where they stand. They said it was the breath of the fire-spirits, the djinn. The name is the warning, and the warning is true: when the poison wind rises, get down, cover your face, and wait for it to pass.

n: The manchineel — arbol de la muerte
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (coastal Caribbean / Florida / C. & S. America — diffuse)
ctx: Caribbean & tropical American coasts · poison tree; 1521
det: Caribbean & tropical American coasts · poison tree; 1521 — This is the tree the upas only pretended to be. It grows on Caribbean beaches looking like an ordinary shade tree with small green apples, and every part of it is poison: the sap raises blisters where it touches you, the sweet fruit burns the mouth and can kill, and if you stand under it in the rain the runoff scalds your skin. Burning the wood can blind you. The people here painted red rings on the trunks to warn each other. It killed Ponce de Leon with a single poisoned arrow. When you see the red ring, do not touch the tree, and do not shelter under it.

n: The mandrake — the screaming root
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Mediterranean & Central Asia — diffuse)
det: Mediterranean & Central Asia · folklore — They said the mandrake root, shaped like a little man, would scream when you tore it from the ground, and that the scream would kill anyone who heard it — so you tied a dog to the root, stopped your ears, and let the dog pull it out and die in your place. The root does not scream. But it is a true poison — the same nightshade family as belladonna, full of the drugs that in a small dose put a patient to sleep for surgery and in a large one bring delirium, then coma, then death. The scream was how they remembered that a plant this useful was also this deadly.

n: The Erlking — the alder-king
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Germanic/Danish forests — Saale valley, Thuringia representative; diffuse)
det: Germanic/Danish forests · folklore; Goethe 1782 — A father rides home through the woods at night with a sick child in his arms, and the child cries out that a crowned figure is beside them, calling him, reaching for him — and the father sees only mist and hears only the wind, and says hush, and rides on, and the boy is dead when they arrive. That is the Erlking, the king of the alders, who takes children in the dark forest. Read plainly, it is a fever, and the woods at night, and a death a parent cannot ride fast enough to outrun. The name itself is an old mistake — an elf-king misheard as a tree — but the fear underneath it is exact.

n: The yakshi's pala tree
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Kerala, India — diffuse)
det: Kerala, India · folklore — In Kerala there is a tree, the pala, whose small white flowers open at night and throw a thick sweet scent, and the story is that a yakshi waits beneath it in the shape of a beautiful woman who asks a passing man for a little lime, and takes him, and he is found dead under the tree at dawn. To rest under the pala at night is said to be suicide. There are true reasons a person learns to keep clear of it in the dark: its milky sap draws snakes into the branches, and its scent alone can sicken. The demon is beautiful; the tree is genuinely a bad place to sleep.

n: The churchyard yew — the death-tree
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (British churchyards — Fortingall representative; diffuse)
det: British churchyards (Fortingall) · tradition — The dark tree in the churchyard, older than the church and often older than the parish, is a real poison: leaves, bark, and seed all carry it, enough in a double handful of needles to kill a grown person, enough to kill the cattle that graze it — which is exactly why it was planted among the graves, to keep the livestock off the dead. In hot weather the poison rises off it as a vapour, and the old warning against sleeping in its shade is not entirely wrong. It is the tree of death and, being evergreen, of resurrection too. Do not eat any part of it. Do not let the animals graze beneath it.

n: The gympie-gympie — the suicide plant
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (NE Queensland rainforest, Australia; also PNG/Indonesia — diffuse)
det: NE Queensland, Australia · stinging tree — There is a soft, fuzzy-leaved shrub in the Queensland rainforest that hikers learn to fear more than the snakes. Its leaves are covered in tiny glass needles that inject a venom close kin to a scorpion's, and the lightest brush feels like being set on fire and electrocuted together — a pain that fades over hours only to flare again for months, woken by cold or a shower. You do not even have to touch it: the needles drift on the air. They call it the suicide plant because the pain has driven both animals and people to it. Learn the leaf. Do not brush the fuzzy shrub.

n: The asking rivers — the Dart & Peg O'Nell's Ribble
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (British rivers — the Dart (Devon), the Ribble (Lancashire); diffuse)
det: Dart (Devon) & Ribble (Lancashire), England · folklore — Some rivers, the old country says, take a life on a schedule. The Dart on Dartmoor claims a heart every year — 'cruel Dart, every year thou claim'st a heart' — and when it is ready it cries out; and the cry is true, for it is the sound the river makes in dangerous flood, exactly when it is most likely to drown someone. The Ribble's Peg wants a life every seventh year, and if no beast is drowned in her place, a person is. Under the ghosts is a real accounting: a river in spate will take someone, and the story is how a village remembers not to be the one it takes.

n: St Elian's cursing well — Ffynnon Elian
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Llanelian-yn-Rhos, North Wales)
det: Llanelian-yn-Rhos, North Wales · cursing well; 1700s–1850s — Most wells here were for healing; this one, for a while, was for cursing. At Ffynnon Elian in the late 1700s you could pay the keeper to write your enemy's name on a scrap of slate and sink it in the water with a curse, and the word would go round, and your enemy — hearing he had been 'put in the well' — might take to his bed and waste and sometimes die, unless he paid, more, to have the slate fished out and the curse lifted. The water did nothing. The fear did everything: a whole economy of dread and extortion run out of a saint's spring, until the law shut it down. A holy well turned exactly inside out.

n: Fugu — the forbidden liver
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Japan — Shimonoseki representative; diffuse)
det: Japan (Shimonoseki) · tradition; 1975 poisoning — The pufferfish is one of the great delicacies of Japan and one of the surest ways to die at dinner: it carries a nerve poison a thousand times deadlier than cyanide, with no antidote, that leaves you paralysed and fully awake while you stop breathing. The poison sits in the organs, and the whole elaborate ritual around fugu — the years of training, the licence, the forbidden liver, the poisoned parts locked away and burned — is one long safety rule written as a taboo. The people who die are the ones who break it: the fisherman who cleaned his own catch, the famous actor who swore he was immune and ate the liver anyway.

n: Bitter cassava — the processing rule
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (sub-Saharan Africa 'konzo belt' & Amazonia — diffuse)
ctx: sub-Saharan Africa & Amazonia · konzo belt; processing rule
det: sub-Saharan Africa & Amazonia · konzo belt; processing rule — The root that feeds half a billion people is, in its hardiest form, full of cyanide, and the long traditional way of preparing it — peel, grate, soak three days and throw the water away, dry it, cook it — is not cooking, it is detoxification, and everyone who grows it is taught that the steps are not optional. The trap springs in famine: when the drought kills every crop but the bitter cassava, and the family is too hungry to wait out the soak, they eat it early, and the cyanide that is left brings on konzo — a sudden, permanent paralysis of the legs. The rule is oldest and most binding exactly where hunger makes it hardest to keep.

n: Ackee — eat it when it yawns
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Jamaica; also W. Africa, Caribbean, Florida — diffuse)
det: Jamaica · Jamaican vomiting sickness — Jamaica's national dish begins with a fruit that will poison you if you take it too soon. The rule is simple and old: never pick the ackee, wait until it ripens on the branch and splits open by itself — 'yawns,' they say — because the closed, unripe fruit is loaded with a toxin that crashes your blood sugar and can kill a child in an afternoon. As the fruit opens, the poison drains away, so the yawning pod is not just a sign of ripeness, it is the all-clear. The people who get sick are the ones who force it open early, usually in the lean months when the fruit comes before its time.

n: The glowing sea — red-tide shellfish
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Pacific NW coast — Salish Sea representative; global temperate coasts — diffuse)
det: Pacific NW coast · PSP; 1793 account — On some summer nights the sea lights up where it breaks, a cold blue glow in the wave, and along this coast the old rule was to leave the shellfish alone when it did. The glow is a bloom, and some blooms load the clams and mussels with a paralysing poison that cooking will not destroy and that leaves the shellfish looking and tasting exactly right — so the first sign of trouble is your own lips going numb, and then your chest. The reading was never perfect; the poison can be there without the light. But long before anyone could test the water, the glowing sea was the warning, and the warning was mostly right.

n: Aconite — the queen of poisons
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (mountainous N Hemisphere — Alps/Europe representative; diffuse)
det: N Hemisphere mountains · poison plant — The pretty hooded flower on the mountain path is the queen of poisons. Every part of it, most of all the root, carries a toxin that stops the nerves and the heart within hours, that a couple of milligrams can kill, that has no antidote — and that soaks in through the skin, so you should not so much as pull it up bare-handed. Its names are the warning written down: wolfsbane, because it was painted on arrows to drop wolves; the dart-plant, for the same; the witch's flower; the poison Rome used for executions. When a plant collects that many names for killing, believe them, and do not touch it.

n: The bear whose name you don't say
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (boreal/circumpolar — Fennoscandia/Siberia representative; diffuse)
det: boreal / circumpolar · taboo tradition — In the great northern forests the most dangerous animal was the one whose name you did not say, because to say it might call it to you. So the true word for the bear was let go, and in its place people used careful nicknames — the brown one, the honey-eater, the shaggy one, mead-paw, wide-brow, grandfather — each culture choosing its own, none of them the real name. Farther south, where the bear was no great threat, the plain old word survived untouched. The nickname was fear and respect at once: you fed the apex predator a polite name so it would leave you, your camp, and your children alone.

n: The whistle you don't blow
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (cross-cultural / global — Arctic/Sapmi representative for the aurora form; diffuse)
det: Arctic / Sápmi · aurora taboo — Almost everywhere, there is a time and place you must not whistle. Not on the ship, where a whistle is said to raise the wind and bring the storm. Not in the mine. Not at night, when it calls up spirits or snakes or, in Mexico, the owl-witch. Not under the northern lights, which the Sami say may hear you and reach down and take you. Beneath the spirits there is a plain truth: a whistle travels a long way through quiet dark and tells everything with ears exactly where you are — and in the old theatres, where sailors worked the rigging, a wrong whistle was the cue that dropped a wall of scenery. The rule against a careless whistle is older and more practical than it sounds.

n: Hákarl — the fresh shark you must not eat
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Iceland; Greenland shark, N Atlantic)
det: Iceland · tradition (Greenland shark) — Iceland's fermented shark is a lesson in patience enforced as a rule: the Greenland shark, fresh, will make you sick as a drunk and worse, because its flesh is soaked in urea and a compound that turns to ammonia. You cannot cook that out. What works is time — bury the meat in gravel under stones for months, let it ferment, then hang it to dry until the poison has drained and gassed away. The eye-watering ammonia stink that everyone jokes about is the proof the shark is safe. Eat it fresh and you break the only rule that makes it food.

n: Poison hemlock — the philosopher's death
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Europe/Mediterranean — Greece representative; now widespread)
det: Greece / Europe · poison; Socrates 399 BC — The plant that killed Socrates looks like wild carrot — feathery leaves, little white flower-heads, growing in the damp field edge — and that is the danger. Its poison, concentrated in the root and seeds, quietly paralyses from the feet up until the chest stops; a handful of leaves is enough. It grows cheek by jowl with the carrots and parsnips and parsley it resembles, which is why the old rule is blunt: do not eat a wild plant of the carrot family unless you are certain what it is. The pretty white umbel is not always dinner. Sometimes it is the cup they gave the philosopher.

n: Water hemlock — the sweet deadly root
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (North America — representative; also Europe)
det: North America · poison plant — The most violently poisonous plant on the continent grows at the water's edge and looks like a wild parsnip — and worse, its root actually tastes sweet and pleasant, which is how it kills the foragers who trust it. A cut in the stem weeps a brown liquid that is nearly pure convulsant; a small bite brings seizures within the hour, and often death. The old rule is the only safe one: near water, do not dig and eat a carrot-family root you cannot name with certainty. The plant that offers you a sweet parsnip by the stream may be handing you a seizure you do not wake from.

n: Oleander — the beautiful poison hedge
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Mediterranean native; planted worldwide — diffuse)
det: Mediterranean; planted worldwide · poison hedge — The flowering hedge along the road, in a thousand warm-country gardens, is one of the deadliest ornamentals there is. Every part of the oleander — leaf, flower, stem, seed, sap — carries a heart poison of the same family as the drug that steadies and, in excess, stops a heartbeat. It does not lose its poison when it dries; its smoke is dangerous to breathe; people have been sickened by roasting food on its cut sticks and by honey from its flowers. What mostly saves us is that it tastes bitter enough to spit out. Admire the flowers. Do not burn the wood, skewer your food on it, or let the children chew it.

n: Datura — the devil's weed
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (temperate/tropical worldwide — diffuse)
det: worldwide temperate/tropical · the devil's weed — The thorn-apple with the white trumpet flower is called the devil's weed for cause: every part of it is loaded with the deliriant drugs of the nightshade family, and there is no telling how strong any one plant is, so the dose that gives visions and the dose that kills sit invisibly close together. Those who take it for the visions wake — if they wake — remembering monsters. It seeds itself among the wheat and the beans and has poisoned whole villages through the flour. The old dread of it is the right instinct: this is a plant whose poison you cannot measure, and so must not gamble on.

n: Belladonna — the sweet berry, deadly nightshade
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Europe/W Asia — Central Europe representative; diffuse)
det: Europe & W Asia · deadly nightshade — Its name means 'beautiful woman', for the drops that Renaissance ladies put in their eyes to widen the pupils — the very same poison. But deadly nightshade earns its other name at the berry: glossy, black, cherry-sweet, and set at a child's eye level, so a small handful can kill the child who thinks they've found fruit. The whole plant is loaded with the drugs of its family, named for the Fate who cuts the thread of life. The dread around it is exact: the sweetest-tasting berry in the hedge is one of the deadliest, and a child cannot tell. Teach them this berry by name, and to leave it.

n: Do not name the dead
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Aboriginal Australia representative; near-universal — diffuse)
det: Aboriginal Australia · taboo — In much of Aboriginal Australia, and in scattered cultures the world over, you do not say the name of someone who has just died. You call them 'that old aunty', or a stand-in name, or nothing; even their photograph is put away. The reason given is that the name might call the spirit back — but the deeper reason is plain to anyone who has grieved: the name of the newly dead is a wound, and not-saying it is how a family keeps from tearing the wound open, day after day, until time has done its slow work. It is a rule that protects the living, dressed as a rule about the dead.

n: The counting taboo
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Levant/Biblical & E Africa representative; widespread — diffuse)
det: Levant & East Africa · taboo — Do not count the children, the herd, the money, the catch — count a thing and you draw the evil eye, and lose it. Scripture would not let Israel be numbered without a ransom paid, and when a king counted his people a plague followed. Herders on the African highlands were taught not to tally their animals at all, but to know each one by face and horn, so they would see at a glance which was missing. Under the superstition is hard sense: the head-count was always the prelude to the tax and the army levy, and to name your exact wealth aloud is to invite the envious and tempt the fates. Some things you are wiser to know without numbering.

n: Do not look back
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Greek & Levantine myth — Mediterranean representative; diffuse)
det: Greek & Levantine myth · Orpheus / Lot — Two of the oldest stories share one rule: do not look back. Orpheus, leading his wife up out of the land of the dead, is told not to turn until they reach the light — and at the last step he turns, and she is gone. Lot's wife, fleeing the burning city, looks back at the home she is losing and is turned to salt. This is not a poison or a place; it is a rule about the pull of the past. Cross the threshold out of disaster and keep walking. Do not turn to look at the fire, the grave, the life you are leaving — the looking is how you lose the way out.

n: Cold iron
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Celtic Britain & Ireland representative; European — diffuse)
det: Celtic Britain & Ireland · folklore — The horseshoe over the door and the iron nail in the threshold are the worn-down remains of an old certainty: that the fairies, the witches, the restless dead cannot cross cold iron. Two true things sit under the charm. One is memory — the Fair Folk who fear iron may be the shadow of an older people, driven into the hills and the burial mounds by newcomers who carried iron weapons the old world could not answer. The other is plain: iron was the hardest, surest, most precious thing a household owned. To ring your home in it against the dark was, in every sense that mattered, to arm yourself with the best you had.

n: Spilled salt
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Renaissance Europe / Mediterranean representative; diffuse)
det: Renaissance Europe · superstition — Spill the salt and a shadow crosses the table; the cure is a pinch tossed over the left shoulder, into the eyes of the Devil who waits behind you. The dread is old and, at bottom, practical. Salt once paid wages and traded like silver, so to spill it was to spill money. More than that, salt was the thing that kept meat and fish from rotting through the winter — the difference, some years, between eating and not — and a purifier besides, which is why it turns up warding off evil and blessing water. A pinch wasted was worth a small ritual of dread. The magic is the memory of how much salt was worth.

n: The forbidden animal — taboo as conservation
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Aotearoa/NZ rahui representative; global — diffuse)
det: Aotearoa/NZ (rāhui) · tradition — Not every forbidding takes; some give. Up and down the coasts of Aotearoa a chief could lay a rahui over a bay or a river — a sacred ban, marked with a post — and no one would take the fish or the eels until the stock had come back. In African forests there are monkeys and buffalo a clan may not hunt because they are its own kin, and so they live; in Himalayan valleys there are groves no axe may touch, and inside them the last of the musk deer survive. Call a thing sacred and untouchable and you have, without a single law, built it a reserve. Of all the forbiddings, this is the one that feeds the future.

n: Huracán / Guabancex — the storm that named itself
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Caribbean — Hispaniola/Greater Antilles representative; also Maya, Guatemala)
det: Caribbean (Taíno) · storm deity; 1498 — The word we use for the worst storm on Earth is the name of a god. To the Taíno of the Caribbean, the hurricane was Guabancex — the raging aspect of the mother-goddess — who when angered sent her two servants ahead of her: one to bring the screaming wind and thunder, one to gather the mountain rains and loose them as a killing flood. Her idol shows a furious face with two arms spiralling out in an S, which is to say the Taíno had drawn the spiral of the storm centuries before anyone saw one from above. Their word for her fury, juracán, is the word you still use: hurricane.

n: Typhon — the hundred-headed storm under the mountain
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Mount Etna, Sicily — his prison; storms of the E Mediterranean)
det: Mount Etna, Sicily · Greek myth — If the hurricane is a goddess, the typhoon is a monster. The Greeks gave the killing storm-wind a body — Typhon, a hundred-headed serpent-giant so vast his head touched the stars, who nearly beat Zeus himself before he was struck down and buried under Mount Etna. From under the mountain he does two terrible things at once: he makes the volcano rage, and he sends out the storm-winds that wreck ships and drown sailors. His name, passing through half the languages of the storm-belt, became the word for the great wind of the eastern seas: typhoon.

n: The tornado — Red Horse and the Cyclone Person
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Great Plains — Oklahoma/Kiowa representative; many Plains nations)
det: Great Plains (Kiowa), US · tradition — The land that gets most of the world's tornadoes had names for them long before the storm-chasers came. To the Kiowa the twister was Red Horse — a sky-being with a horse's body and a serpent's tail, who dropped that tail to the earth to make the funnel, and who, because it understood their speech, could sometimes be talked out of its fury. Other Plains nations knew the Cyclone Person with its thrashing hair, or named the double funnel 'Dead Man Walking.' Look at how they drew it — spirals, dipping tails, rising serpents — and you are looking at the shape of the storm, set down by people who respected it.

n: The Wild Hunt — the storm as a host of the dead
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Northern Europe — Germanic/Norse representative; pan-European)
det: Northern Europe · Germanic/Norse folklore — In the old winters of northern Europe, the gale that howled over the roof at midwinter was a hunt — a host of ghostly riders and baying hounds storming across the sky, led by Odin or by some dark huntsman, the wind their horses' breath and the thunder their passage. You heard it more than saw it, and that was mercy, because to see the Wild Hunt was to be marked: it rode ahead of war and plague, and it gathered up the souls of the newly dead. The winter storm, given riders — and told as an omen you prayed to only hear.

n: The dust devil — the dead walking in a column of dust
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Colorado Plateau / SW US — Navajo representative; also Australia, Africa)
det: SW US (Navajo) · tradition — The little whirlwind that wanders the desert, spinning up dust and dying away, is read across the dry world not as weather but as a ghost. To the Navajo it is the chindi — the bad part of a person, the anger and sickness that could not be carried into harmony, set loose at the last breath. You watch which way it turns: clockwise and it is harmless, counterclockwise and it is the dead walking, and you go around it. It cannot really hurt you the way a poison or a flood can. But it is the shape a restless spirit takes when it crosses open country, and people have always known to let it pass.

n: The Chimera — the mountain that breathes fire
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Yanartaş, Lycia, SW Turkey)
ctx: Yanartaş, Lycia, SW Turkey · eternal flames; myth
det: Yanartaş, Lycia, SW Turkey · eternal flames; myth — A cluster of flames has burned on a Turkish hillside for two and a half thousand years, fed by gas seeping out of the rock and catching fire in the air. The ancient Greeks knew exactly what a mountain that breathes fire must be: the Chimera, the fire-breathing beast that terrorised the coast. And when they said the hero Bellerophon killed it, the old scholars understood the real meaning — he made the burning slope livable. The monster was the vent. Slaying it meant learning to live beside a mountain that would not stop burning.

n: The cenote — the mouth of the underworld
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Yucatán — Chichén Itzá Sacred Cenote representative)
det: Yucatán (Chichén Itzá) · Maya; 1566 account — In the Yucatán the rivers run underground, and where the limestone roof falls in, the water shows as a cenote — a round well opening straight down into the dark. The Maya knew these for exactly what they are: mouths of the underworld, and the dwelling of the rain-god who climbs out of them with the rains. The water was life; the drop was death. At Chichén Itzá the great cenote was the Well of Sacrifice, where in the killing droughts people cast in gold and jade and, at the worst, lives, to beg the rain back. A sinkhole that was at once the water they drank and the door they most feared.

n: The blue hole — the Lusca in the drowned cave
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Andros, Bahamas representative; Belize, Yucatán)
det: Andros, Bahamas · Lusca folklore — Off the coast of Andros, the sea is pocked with blue holes — sinkholes in the seabed that drop into flooded caves, dark and current-torn and deadly to the careless diver. The fishermen say something lives down there: the Lusca, a monstrous thing half shark and half octopus, that reaches up to drag down boats and swimmers and does not give them back. Strip away the tentacles and you find the truth the elders were teaching: the hole itself is the killer, and the monster is the warning — a body given to a dangerous place so the young would keep away from it.

n: The Boiling Lake — the giant's cauldron
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Dominica — Valley of Desolation)
det: Dominica · 1901 discovery — High in a dead volcanic valley on Dominica, a lake sits at a permanent boil — a flooded crack over the magma, its water churning grey-blue at near-boiling under a permanent hood of steam, in a valley so full of vented gas that nothing grows. The Kalinago called it the giant's cooking-pot, and a door to the underworld. It is not only a story: the gas that hisses from the ground has killed the people who came too close. A cauldron the Earth keeps on the boil, in a valley named Desolation for good reason.

n: The Door to Hell — the fire we lit and cannot put out
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Karakum Desert, Turkmenistan)
det: Darvaza, Karakum, Turkmenistan · burning since 1971 — The most famous burning hole on Earth is not a natural wonder; it is a mistake nobody could take back. In 1971, the story goes, Soviet drillers in the Turkmen desert punched into a pocket of gas, the ground caved in, and — to keep the methane from poisoning the villages — they set it on fire, sure it would burn out in a week. It is still burning. Hundreds of flames stand in a pit you can feel the heat of from the rim, fed by an underground sea of gas that may never run dry. The Chimera's mountain caught fire on its own, thousands of years ago. This one we lit, and cannot put out, and named the Door to Hell because that is what it looks like.

n: Lusi — the mud that swallowed the towns
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Porong, Sidoarjo, East Java, Indonesia)
det: Sidoarjo, East Java, Indonesia · since 2006 — It came up in a rice field one morning in 2006 and it never stopped. Hot mud, rising out of the ground faster than anyone could dam it, spreading until it had swallowed thirteen villages whole — houses, factories, schools, a highway — under forty metres of grey ooze, and driven sixty thousand people off their own land. Twenty years on it is still rising, held back only by walls the government keeps building higher. Whether the drillers next door woke it or an earthquake did is still argued, and the argument is convenient, because while the cause stays uncertain the bill stays unpaid. The earth had the mud in it all along. Something opened a door and let it out.

n: Genbaku Dome — the ruin kept as the flash left it
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Hiroshima, Japan)
det: Hiroshima, Japan · 1945 — One building was left standing beneath the blast, almost directly below where the bomb went off, and they have kept it exactly as the flash left it — the dome stripped to its iron ribs, the walls cracked and somehow holding. Everything around it was rebuilt. This was not. It is left broken on purpose, because a city that tidied away the morning of 6 August 1945 would let people forget what a single bomb did to it in a second, and the ruin refuses to let the sentence be finished cleanly. Grief refusing tidiness, so the record stays legible.

n: Trinity — the journey of the dead man
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Jornada del Muerto, White Sands, New Mexico)
ctx: Jornada del Muerto, New Mexico, US · 1945
det: Jornada del Muerto, New Mexico, US · 1945 — At 5:29 in the morning on 16 July 1945, in a stretch of New Mexico desert the Spanish had already named the Jornada del Muerto — the journey of the dead man — the first atomic bomb went off, and the sand beneath it fused into a pale green glass they called trinitite. Nothing had ever burned that hot on the ground of this planet. The men who built it watched from behind earthworks and argued for the rest of their lives about what they had opened. The site is unlocked to visitors two days a year; the other three hundred and sixty-three, the door to the nuclear age stays shut behind a fence.

n: Point Nemo — the absence of everywhere else
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (oceanic pole of inaccessibility, South Pacific)
ctx: South Pacific · oceanic pole of inaccessibility
det: South Pacific · oceanic pole of inaccessibility — It is the farthest you can get from land in any direction — more than two and a half thousand kilometres of open water on every side, in a place so empty that the nearest human beings are usually not on the Earth at all, but overhead, four hundred kilometres up, in the station passing over. Almost nothing lives in the water; it sits beneath the dead centre of the least living ocean there is. And it is where the space agencies aim their dead spacecraft to come down and burn, so the seabed below is a scrapyard of fallen stations and cargo ships. A place with no quality of its own — defined entirely by the absence of everywhere else.

n: Ngogo — the chimpanzees went to war twice
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Ngogo, Kibale National Park, Uganda)
det: Kibale NP, Uganda · chimp war 2015–2018 — It was the largest community of chimpanzees anyone had ever found — a hundred and sixty-six of them, where fifty is normal — and for ten years they made themselves larger the only way they knew, by killing the neighbours one at a time and taking their ground. Then, having won, they came apart. By 2015 the group was splitting; by 2018 the break was final, and the two halves began raiding and killing across the new line between them, twenty-eight of their own dead so far. A chimpanzee civil war has been watched twice in the history of the science — once here, and once at Gombe by Goodall. It may come once in five centuries, and we have now seen both.

n: Gorongosa — the war is still selecting
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Gorongosa National Park, Mozambique)
ctx: Gorongosa NP, Mozambique · war 1977–1992; tuskless legacy
det: Gorongosa NP, Mozambique · war 1977–1992; tuskless legacy — Two armies paid for a fifteen-year war by selling ivory, and nine of every ten large animals in this park died for it. The ones that lived were the ones with nothing worth taking — the tuskless. Before the war, fewer than one female in five was born without tusks; among the survivors it was half. And this is the part that does not stop when the shooting does: the gene that spares the daughters sits on the X chromosome, and it is lethal to sons, so every tuskless mother's male calf dies before it is born. The war ended in 1992. The thing it set in motion is still deciding, calf by calf, who gets to exist.

n: Minkébé — six kilometres from a road
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Minkébé National Park, northern Gabon)
det: northern Gabon · elephant collapse 2004–2014 — The forest kept them safe for a hundred years — it is so thick you can lose the trail by stepping off it — and while elephants were being shot everywhere else in Africa, these ones were fine. Then a road went in, on the far side of the border. The south of the park lies fifty-eight kilometres from the nearest Gabonese road, and its elephants are only thinned; the north comes within six kilometres of Cameroon's national highway, and the north is empty. Twenty-five thousand of them in ten years, the tusks carried up that road to the coast. Gabon doubled the park's budget and it made no difference at all. The road was in someone else's country.

n: Lobéké — the road that will stop the poaching
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Lobéké National Park, SE Cameroon; Sangha Trinational)
ctx: SE Cameroon · poaching front; road c. 2001
det: SE Cameroon · poaching front; road c. 2001 — The Baka hunted this forest for thousands of years, and in 2001 it became a national park and they were put outside it — UNESCO's own listing admits their hunting and gathering is no longer allowed, and that this breeds conflict; Survival International has recorded beatings, expulsions and deaths at the hands of the park's own guards. A boy of eighteen, asked about the place, said: we would rather die than go there. And now the government wants to run a road along the river — to stop the poaching, it says. The last road Cameroon ran beside a rainforest park came within six kilometres of Minkébé, and Minkébé lost twenty-five thousand elephants in ten years.

n: The Dunstable Swan — the last badge of belonging
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Dominican Friary findspot, Dunstable, Bedfordshire, England)
det: Dunstable, Bedfordshire, England · c. 1400; found 1965 — The great houses handed these out to mark who was theirs — a man wearing your badge was your man, in the feasts and the killings alike — and the old treasure-rolls list them by the hundred: a gold swan enamelled white, a little chain at the neck, two ounces of gold apiece. Every one of them is gone but this, turned up in a friary field at Dunstable. The swan is on it because the Bohuns had told themselves they were descended from a knight who came ashore in a boat pulled by one. A token of belonging, from an age when belonging to the wrong lord could see you hanged for it.

n: Cuerdale — the return that never came
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (south bank of the Ribble, Cuerdale, near Preston, Lancashire)
ctx: near Preston, Lancashire, England · buried c. 905; found 1840
det: near Preston, Lancashire, England · buried c. 905; found 1840 — Forty kilos of silver in a lead chest, three feet down on a bend of the river — a war-chest, buried by men driven out of Dublin who fully meant to come back for it. They never came back. The coins in it are from Wessex and York and Rome and Byzantium and Samarkand, and most of the jewellery had been hacked into weighable scraps rather than worn. Someone is said to have planted a willow to mark the spot; the willow died. And the silver lay there for a thousand years, waiting for owners who were dead, until a work-gang mending the embankment struck the chest by accident.

n: Sandur — the grave that had to be deeper
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (churchyard, Sandur, Sandoy, Faroe Islands)
det: Sandoy, Faroe Islands · medieval grave; 1080s — Two plague dead needed a deeper grave than the ground usually gave, and at the bottom of the extra depth the diggers struck the altar of the very first church built on this spot, and ninety-eight silver coins hidden beneath it — half of them German, some from the Middle East, the only hoard ever found in these islands. Six churches have stood here, each raised on the ruin of the last. In 1988 the newest one caught fire and every piece of silver inside it melted to nothing. The only silver left is the silver they buried, and forgot, and turned up by accident under two plague victims.

n: Schlachthof-Fünf — where you leave your coat
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (former Dresden slaughterhouse, now Messe Dresden)
det: Dresden, Germany · 1945 — He lived because the Germans had nowhere to put their prisoners but the cellar of a slaughterhouse, and the cellar was deep enough. Above him, on the night of 13 February 1945, the city burned in a firestorm the raid had been designed to build. He wrote home that it had killed a quarter of a million people in a day; the true number is argued to this day, and the figures people offer are ten times apart. The building is a convention centre now. The cold meat-locker where he came up alive into a dead city is where the visitors leave their coats.

n: Watership Down — every place in it is real
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Watership Down, Ecchinswell, Hampshire, England)
det: Hampshire, England · novel, 1972 — The book is about rabbits driven off their home by men with machines, and the writer swears in his opening note that every place in it is real — a real hill, a real farm, the footbridges over the Test exactly where the rabbits found them. On the first pages their warren is destroyed for a housing estate. Six years after the book came out, a man of great wealth bought the land below the hill and turned the crest into a racehorse gallop. The stud on the down where the warren was dug out and gassed is called, without any apparent irony, Watership Down.

n: Mount Greylock — where we first made it snow
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Mount Greylock, western Massachusetts)
det: western Massachusetts, US · cloud-seeding 1946–47 — On a November day in 1946 a plane climbed out of upstate New York, chased a cloud sixty miles east over this mountain, and dropped six pounds of dry ice into it — and it snowed. The first snow that human beings ever made. The man who improved the trick, discovering that silver iodide seeds ice even better, was Bernard Vonnegut; and the military that funded him soon had him seeding clouds from the same model of bomber that had firebombed his own brother over Dresden, and nudged a hurricane that swerved into Savannah. Bernard's kid brother Kurt worked the public-relations desk, listened to Bernie describe a crystal that teaches water how to freeze, and wrote Cat's Cradle — in which exactly such a crystal, ice-nine, touches the sea and freezes every drop of water on Earth. The place where we learned to make the weather is also where somebody first imagined us ending the world by accident, one seed crystal at a time.

n: Octopolis & Octlantis — the war on the pile of shells
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Jervis Bay, NSW, Australia)
det: Jervis Bay, NSW, Australia · found 2009 & 2017 — We like to say the octopus is the nearest thing to an intelligent alien — a mind that grew up entirely apart from us, six hundred million years down its own road. On a pile of shells in an Australian bay, two little clusters of them live crammed together, and here is what the alien mind does with company: it fights. They grapple at the den mouths and haul each other out by the arms; they flush a furious dark colour and stand tall to loom over a rival; and one will reach down, gather a fistful of silt and broken shell, and hurl it — aimed — at the neighbour who won't keep his distance. Evolution built a second intelligence on this planet, out of a mollusc, in the dark, with nothing in common with us at all. And one of the first things it did with others of its kind was learn to throw things at them.

n: Coober Pedy — the field of a quarter-million holes
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Coober Pedy, South Australia)
ctx: Coober Pedy, South Australia · opal fields since 1915
det: Coober Pedy, South Australia · opal fields since 1915 — The opal made the town live underground, and it left the surface a minefield. Over a century of digging, prospectors have sunk something like a quarter of a million shafts into this plain, straight down — most of them narrow, unmarked, unfenced, and simply abandoned when the flash of colour ran out. So the ground around Coober Pedy is pocked with holes a person can drop through without warning, which is why the signs at the edge of town do not say beware of anything in particular; they say, simply, DO NOT WALK BACKWARDS. And it is not only people who fall. The shafts trap the desert's animals too — kangaroos, birds, small marsupials — dropping into the dark with no way to climb out. A whole landscape turned, by the hunt for something beautiful, into a field of open graves.

n: The Beast of Gévaudan — a hundred throats in the Age of Reason
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Margeride Mountains, Gévaudan — Lozère/Haute-Loire, Massif Central, south-central France; cf. R037 Loire-basin margin)
det: Margeride, Lozère, France · 1764–1767 — For three years, something came out of the woods of the Margeride and tore the throats out of shepherd children. Around a hundred people died, most of them women and girls minding animals alone on the high pastures. King Louis XV sent his own gun-bearer, who shot a large wolf, had it stuffed, carried it to Versailles, and was paid as the man who had ended it — and then the killing went on for another eighteen months, while the court, having declared victory, looked away. A local farmer named Jean Chastel finally shot the thing in 1767. No one ever agreed what it was: a wolf, a pack, a wolf-dog someone was keeping, a striped hyena — or, the word the villagers used from the first week, a loup-garou, a man who had traded his soul for the right to hunt his neighbours. The silver bullet came later, added by a novelist. The true story is plainer, and worse: in the Age of Reason, in the middle of France, the poor were eaten while the powerful posed with the wrong corpse.

n: The white cannibal — the man who eats the taken
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (diffuse — West & West-Central African coast; Loango/Kongo & Cape Lopez/Gabon representative; West-Central Africans were the plurality of the enslaved)
ctx: West-Central African coast · slave-trade era folklore
det: West-Central African coast · slave-trade era folklore — From the African shore it was obvious what the pale men in the ships were. They came across the water, they gave cloth and iron for human beings, they packed the people into the dark of the hold, and they sailed away — and not one of the taken ever came back. Where did they all go? The answer the coast arrived at, over and over for three hundred years, was the plainest one: the strangers were eating them. They said the whites ground the bones into gunpowder, pressed the blood into their red wine, boiled the bodies down into oil and cheese, and that everything that came off those ships was made, somehow, of the missing. It was not literally true. But as a description of what the trade actually did — consume people without end, and turn them into goods — it was exact. The Europeans called the Africans cannibals to justify taking them. The Africans looked at the ships, and knew better.

n: Kaffa — where the Slavs became the word for slave
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Kaffa / Feodosia, Crimea; the raids ranged across Ukraine / Poland-Lithuania / S Russia — diffuse)
det: Kaffa (Feodosia), Crimea · slave raids, 15th–18th c. — For three hundred years the raiders came up out of the south grass every summer, circling the villages like wild geese, and they carried two things: ropes to bind the grown, and baskets to carry off the children, because children were the prize. They called it the harvesting of the steppe. What they harvested was people — a million and more from Ukraine and Poland and southern Russia — driven in coffles to the market at Kaffa on the Crimean shore, where twenty thousand a year were sold across the tables and shipped to the galleys, the harems, and the fields of the Ottoman world. So many of the taken, here and in the older markets before, were of one people that their very name wore down into the name of the condition itself: Slav, sclavus, slave. It is the rawest thing a word can carry — a whole nation's name, ground down over centuries of the market into the word for a thing that can be owned.

n: Drekkingarhylur — where the law drowned women
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Drekkingarhylur / Öxará river, Almannagjá gorge, Þingvellir, SW Iceland; cf. Sacred S4-038 Thingvellir Law Rock — same place, dark side)
det: Þingvellir, Iceland · executions 1618–1749 — The same rift where Iceland once spoke its whole law aloud, from memory, every year, has a quieter pool a little way down the river, beneath a small waterfall, and it has a name: Drekkingarhylur, the drowning pool. After a foreign king and a stern new church rewrote the rules in the 1500s, the free assembly of the north became a place of public death — men beheaded on the spit, witches burned in the gorge, and women, eighteen of them across a century and a half, tied into a sack and pushed under the water and held there until they stopped. Their crime was almost always the same crime: a child born outside a marriage, a love the edict forbade. The men who fathered those same children were beheaded instead, which was reckoned the cleaner death. The pool is half-filled with gravel now, from a bridge they laid across it, and a small sign tells you what it was. It is worth standing there a while, at the beautiful place where the law of a free people learned to drown its daughters.

n: Oak Island — the money pit that keeps its six
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Oak Island, Mahone Bay, South Shore, Nova Scotia, Canada)
ctx: Oak Island, Nova Scotia, Canada · money pit since 1795
det: Oak Island, Nova Scotia, Canada · money pit since 1795 — For two hundred and thirty years a small island in Nova Scotia has been eating men and money and giving back nothing but a story. It began with a boy and a hole and a few rotten oak platforms, and it has grown into the longest treasure hunt in the world — company after company, syndicate after syndicate, a young Franklin Roosevelt among them, every one of them certain that just below the water line lay the pirate gold, or the Templars' Ark, or the lost crown of France. Six men have died for it, in cave-ins and foul gas and a burst boiler, and the legend consoles the survivors with a promise: the island will yield when the seventh man dies. Here is the part the legend leaves out. A geologist went down in the sixties and found no booby-trap tunnels at all — only rock that floods because it is porous rock beside the sea. The oceanographers came and found nothing. In two and a third centuries no one has brought up a single object worth the digging. The curse is real enough; but the treasure was always the hole itself, and the hole only ever wanted the digging. It is the drops file made into a place — proof that a story, if it is good enough, will take everything you have and call the taking a clue.

n: Mauritius — the island that ate its own symbol
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Mauritius, Indian Ocean)
ctx: Mauritius, Indian Ocean · dodo extinct c. 1662
det: Mauritius, Indian Ocean · dodo extinct c. 1662 — The dodo's whole crime was that it had never learned to be afraid, because on Mauritius there had never been anything to fear. It nested on the open ground, laid one egg, and looked up, mildly, at whatever approached — which is how the Dutch found it in 1598, and how, within a single human lifetime, it was gone. The sailors clubbed a few for meat and a few more for sport; but the real executioners came off the ships on four legs and in the bilges — the pigs and rats and cats and monkeys, which found a bird that laid its only egg where anything could reach it. By about 1680 there were none. Then, and only then, did the museums of Europe begin to want one badly — and there were none left to send. We named it after a fool for the sin of trusting us, and made its name our word for a thing so thoroughly dead it will never return. It is the first face of the age of extinctions, and still the truest: not malice, exactly — just a species that walked up to say hello.

n: Eldey — where the last two were killed to order
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Eldey islet, off SW Iceland)
ctx: off SW Iceland (Eldey) · last great auks killed 1844
det: off SW Iceland (Eldey) · last great auks killed 1844 — You can put a date and almost a time on this one, which is rare. On the third of June, 1844, three fishermen rowed out to a bare rock off Iceland called Eldey, because a collector had let it be known he would pay well for a great auk, and great auks were nearly gone. They found two — the last two anyone would ever confirm. They strangled them. One had been sitting on an egg; a man stepped on it. The skins went to a museum, where you can still see them. Here is the accountability, and it is a strange, modern kind: by the end, nobody killed the great auk out of hatred, or even hunger. They killed the last of them because the last of them had become precious — because a thing that is almost gone is worth more than a thing that is merely rare, and worth most of all on the very day it becomes the last. We have not entirely unlearned this. It is worth knowing the name of the rock.

n: Beaumaris Zoo — the tiger that was protected too late
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Tasmania; the last died at Beaumaris Zoo, Hobart)
det: Tasmania · last thylacine died 1936 — For decades the government of Tasmania paid a pound a head to anyone who killed a thylacine, because the striped marsupial was said to be taking the settlers' sheep — a charge that was mostly false, but a pound was a pound. They shot and trapped and poisoned it out of the hills. By 1930 the wild ones were essentially gone; the last one anyone shot was shot that year. The very last thylacine on Earth lived out its days in a cage at the Beaumaris Zoo in Hobart, and on the seventh of September, 1936, it died — not of old age, but because someone left it locked out of its shelter through a night of Tasmanian cold. Fifty-nine days earlier, the government had finally made it illegal to harm a thylacine. There was, by then, exactly one.
