Australia now marks the day it died as Threatened Species Day — which is either the most hopeful thing in this entry or the most damning, depending on how the next century goes.

n: The Yangtze — where the goddess drowned in traffic
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (middle-lower Yangtze River, China)
ctx: middle-lower Yangtze, China · baiji lost c. 2006
det: middle-lower Yangtze, China · baiji lost c. 2006 — For a couple of thousand years the boatmen of the Yangtze believed a pale, nearly-blind dolphin was a goddess who watched over them, and they called her exactly that. She had been in the river for twenty million years. It took about fifty to finish her: the rolling hooks and electric nets that snagged her by accident, the boat engines whose noise drowned out the sonar she saw the world with, the dams, the poison, the sheer relentless traffic of a river carrying a nation's freight. Nobody hunted the baiji. That is the whole horror of it — she was not killed on purpose by anyone; she was used to death by everyone, a casualty of a river doing its job. In 2006 an expedition ran the entire length of her world, from the Three Gorges dam to the sea, listening and looking for six weeks, and found not one. She was the first dolphin humans have ever erased, and the first to go without a villain — which is the kind of extinction the next century will specialize in. Follow the accountability here and it dissolves in your hands, into all of us, going quietly about our business on the water.

n: Monteverde — the first thing the climate switched off
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Monteverde Cloud Forest, Costa Rica; cf. Sacred S5-324)
ctx: Monteverde, Costa Rica · golden toad last seen 1989
det: Monteverde, Costa Rica · golden toad last seen 1989 — It was the colour that got people — a small toad so violently, impossibly orange that the first scientist to see a cluster of them thought someone had spilled paint on the forest floor. The golden toad lived in one narrow band of cloud forest above Monteverde and nowhere else on Earth, breeding in the pools that filled when the mountain mist condensed. In 1987 there were still hundreds. In 1989 there was one — a single male, sitting where the others used to be. Then none. The mist had begun to lift: the warming air pushing the clouds higher up the slope, drying the pools before the tadpoles could finish, while a fungus spreading in the new conditions took the rest. No one shot it or trapped it or paid a bounty for it. It is simply the first creature we can name that the changed climate reached out and switched off, in a forest that still looked untouched. It will not be the last. In an atlas built for a hotter world, this small bright toad is the first entry in a very long ledger.

n: Bramble Cay — the first mammal the sea took
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Bramble Cay / Maizab Kaur, northern Torres Strait)
det: Torres Strait · melomys declared extinct 2016 — On a scrap of sand and coral in the Torres Strait, smaller than a few city blocks and barely higher than the tide, there lived a small brown rat found nowhere else in the world — the only mammal that belonged to the Great Barrier Reef. It asked very little: some low green plants to eat and hide in, and a cay that stayed above the water. The sea took the second thing away. Higher tides and bigger storm surges washed clean over the little island, again and again, killing nearly all the vegetation and, with it, the melomys. By 2009 there were a few. By 2014 there were none, and in 2016 Australia said so out loud. It is a minor animal, and that is exactly why it belongs here: the Bramble Cay melomys is the first mammal we know of that human-driven sea-level rise has erased from the Earth — the first of the drowned. This atlas exists because there will be others. And not all of them will be rats on a sandbar. Some of them will be towns.

n: Aberfan — the mountain of waste that buried the children
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Aberfan, Merthyr Tydfil, South Wales)
det: Aberfan, Merthyr Tydfil, South Wales · 1966 — For fifty years the coal mine piled its waste on the mountain directly above the village, tip on tip, until one of them — Number Seven, built over a spring, on rain-soaked porous rock — did the only thing a waterlogged mountain of slurry can do. On a wet Friday morning in October 1966, just after the children had filed in from assembly singing a hymn about all things bright and beautiful, the tip came down the hill at eighty miles an hour and buried Pantglas Junior School. It killed a hundred and sixteen children — half the children in Aberfan — and twenty-eight adults. The village had warned the Coal Board about those tips for years; a tribunal agreed the disaster was entirely preventable and entirely the Board's fault. Not one person was charged. Not one was fired. And then the Coal Board reached into the fund that strangers around the world had donated for the grieving families, and took a hundred and fifty thousand pounds of it to pay for clearing away the other tips it never should have built. Follow the accountability at Aberfan and it goes all the way to the top, and stops there, having cost no one anything — but a village its children.

n: Benxihu — the deadliest mine, and the sealed door
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Benxihu / Honkeiko colliery, Benxi, Liaoning, NE China)
det: Benxi, Liaoning, China · 1942 — The worst mining disaster the world has ever recorded happened in 1942, in a Manchurian coal pit run by the occupying Japanese and worked by Chinese men who were, in every way that mattered, slaves. When the coal dust caught and the pit filled with fire and gas, more than fifteen hundred of them died — one in three of everyone underground. But the number is not the whole horror. To starve the fire of air, the operators are said to have shut down the ventilation and sealed the entrance, with men still living on the other side of it, and then reported a smaller number than the truth. There is a particular arithmetic that treats the people doing the dying as a cost of keeping the coal coming, and it is the oldest arithmetic in this grim chapter of the atlas. Benxihu is where it reached its highest total. The men are in a mass grave near the pit. Most of their names were never written down — which was, one suspects, the point.

n: Courrières — Europe's worst, and the men left for dead
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Courrières, Pas-de-Calais, northern France)
det: Pas-de-Calais, France · 1906 — In March 1906 a wave of fire ran through the coal galleries under the Pas-de-Calais and killed one thousand and ninety-nine men and boys — still the most any European mine has ever killed at once. The company sent rescuers down, and then — with the pit still burning and the losses already staggering — called them back and began sealing the galleries to save the mine, which meant giving up on anyone still breathing in the dark. Twenty days later, thirteen men who had been written off walked out of the earth on their own. They had felt their way through miles of black tunnel, drunk from puddles, and eaten one of the pit horses to stay alive. A fourteenth came up four days after that. They were living proof that the company had stopped looking too soon — that somewhere in the ledger, the cost of searching had been weighed against the price of the coal, and the coal had won. The miners of northern France drew the obvious conclusion, put down their tools by the tens of thousands, and began the long argument, still not finished, about whose life is worth what.

n: Upper Big Branch — where a coal boss went to prison (for a year)
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Upper Big Branch, Montcoal, Raleigh Co., West Virginia)
det: Raleigh Co., West Virginia, US · 2010 — The Upper Big Branch mine blew up in 2010 and killed twenty-nine men, and for once — genuinely almost the only time — the man at the top went to prison for it. Massey Energy had run the mine on a system of two notebooks: one that recorded the real, damning state of the coal dust and the methane and the broken sprayers, and one, cleaned up, for the federal inspectors, whose approach was radioed ahead so the illegal could be made to look legal for an hour. When the mine exploded, the investigators found all of it. And Don Blankenship, the CEO who had ridden the company for years on the gospel of running coal, running coal, was convicted of conspiring to break the safety laws — the first chief executive of a major American company ever convicted of a workplace-safety crime. He served one year. It was a misdemeanour. The men are remembered by twenty-nine helmets on crosses at the mine mouth; Blankenship, released, declared himself innocent and ran for the United States Senate. Follow the accountability here and, remarkably, it arrives at a cell door — then, just as remarkably, opens it again after twelve months, which the law apparently reckoned the going rate for twenty-nine.

n: Brumadinho — the second dam Vale let fall
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Córrego do Feijão mine, Brumadinho, Minas Gerais, Brazil)
det: Brumadinho, Minas Gerais, Brazil · 2019 — In 2015 a mining-waste dam in Minas Gerais collapsed, killed nineteen people, and poured poison down the Rio Doce all the way to the sea — Brazil's worst environmental disaster, and a warning written in mud. The dam belonged, in part, to a company called Vale. Three years later, on a Friday in January 2019, another Vale dam — this one holding back the sludge from the Córrego do Feijão iron mine at Brumadinho — let go without a siren, and a twelve-million-cubic-metre wave of tailings rolled straight over the mine's own canteen at the lunch hour, and over the offices, and downstream, and killed about two hundred and seventy people, most of them Vale's own workers. The company had known the dam was unsafe; the firm it paid to certify the thing had called it sound. That is what to hold onto at Brumadinho: it was not a bolt from the blue. It was the same company, the same kind of dam, the same waste, the second time — the warning ignored by the very people who had received it in blood three years before. Vale paid seven billion dollars and its executives were charged with homicide. The mud is still there.

n: Soma — 'these things happen,' said the state
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Soma coal mine, Manisa Province, western Turkey)
det: Soma, Manisa, Turkey · 2014 — The Soma mine in western Turkey had been privatised and squeezed — more coal, cheaper coal, the owner proudly cutting the price per tonne — and the squeezing came out of the safety: the gas monitors, the refuge chambers that weren't there, the emergency masks that failed the moment the men finally needed them. When a fire underground turned the air to carbon monoxide in May 2014, three hundred and one of them did not come out. Two weeks earlier, the governing party had voted down a proposal to investigate exactly this mine's safety. And when the grieving crowds gathered, the Prime Minister explained that mine deaths were simply the nature of the work — citing British pit disasters from the eighteen-hundreds, as though a century and a half had taught no one anything — while one of his aides was photographed kicking a mourner already pinned to the ground by soldiers. That photograph is the truest artifact of Soma: not just a company that traded lives for tonnage, but a state that met the funerals with a boot. Some of the men responsible went to prison years later. The kick went unpunished.

n: Gorée — the door the whole world walks through
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Île de Gorée, off Dakar, Senegal)
ctx: Île de Gorée, off Dakar, Senegal · slave-trade era
det: Île de Gorée, off Dakar, Senegal · slave-trade era — More heads of state have wept at Goree than at any other site of the slave trade. They come to the little pink house off Dakar and are led to a doorway that opens straight onto the ocean — the Door of No Return — and told that through it millions of Africans took their last step on their own continent. It is the most powerful three feet of architecture in the whole story of the trade. It is also, historians will quietly tell you, mostly a symbol: the house was largely a merchant's home, the real slave-fort stood down the beach, and the number who actually left through this particular door was probably in the tens of thousands, not the millions. Both things are true, and the atlas keeps both. The mainland ports — Elmina, Ouidah — moved human cargo by the millions and are half-forgotten; Goree moved comparatively few and became the place the world goes to grieve. Sometimes the wound the memory chooses is not the deepest cut but the one you can stand in front of. Goree is where humanity agreed to look. That it may not be where the most died does not make the looking false.

n: Elmina — the oldest door, four centuries of it
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Elmina, Central Region, Ghana; the 'Gold Coast')
ctx: Elmina, Ghana · castle 1482; slave trade to 1800s
det: Elmina, Ghana · castle 1482; slave trade to 1800s — The oldest thing Europe built south of the Sahara is a slave castle, and it is still standing on the Ghanaian coast, whitewashed and enormous, five hundred and forty years old. The Portuguese put it up in 1482 to guard their gold; the name means 'the mine.' When the Dutch took it, the cargo changed from metal to people, and for a century and more some thirty thousand human beings a year were held in its dungeons — two hundred to a cell, in a darkness that rose inch by inch on its own filth until the floor you walk today sits well above the floor they lay on — and then walked out through the small sea-door that the guides, everywhere on this coast, call the Door of No Return. There is a staircase inside Elmina that runs from the governor's bright airy rooms straight down to the women's dungeon, built for one purpose, and it tells you everything about who this architecture served. The gold is long gone. The castle the gold paid for is what remains, and what it remembers is not gold.

n: Bunce Island — the castle that stocked the rice coast
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Bunce Island, Sierra Leone River, upriver of Freetown)
ctx: Bunce Island, Sierra Leone · slave castle, late 17th–18th c.
det: Bunce Island, Sierra Leone · slave castle, late 17th–18th c. — The planters of South Carolina and Georgia grew rice, which they did not themselves know how to grow, so they bought people who did — specifically people from the stretch of West Africa the traders called the Rice Coast, who had cultivated it for generations, and who therefore fetched a premium in Charleston. A great many were shipped from a tiny island in the Sierra Leone River called Bunce, a British slave castle run by a London firm whose senior partner, Richard Oswald, would later sit at the table negotiating the treaty that recognised American independence — a man who helped author the freedom of one new nation while trafficking the un-freedom of tens of thousands into the fields of another. The captives from this coast carried their rice knowledge across the water, and their language, and their spirits, and became the Gullah, who kept more of Africa alive on American soil than anyone. Bunce Island itself is now a ruin swallowed by jungle, visited by almost no one. The Lowcountry it stocked can still be heard speaking, if you listen, in a grammar that came from here.

n: Angola — the plantation that never stopped being one
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Louisiana State Penitentiary, West Feliciana Parish, Louisiana)
ctx: Angola, Louisiana, US · former plantation; prison since 1901
det: Angola, Louisiana, US · former plantation; prison since 1901 — There is a prison in Louisiana where mostly-Black men pick cotton by hand in a field, watched by armed white guards on horseback, and it is not a photograph from 1850 — it is now. The place is called Angola, the largest maximum-security prison in the United States, eighteen thousand acres in a bend of the Mississippi. Before it was a prison it was a slave plantation, assembled by one of the biggest slave traders in American history, and named — this is not a metaphor — for the country in Africa that many of the people enslaved on it were stolen from. When slavery was abolished, the state simply leased the convicts out instead and housed them in the old slave quarters, because the 13th Amendment had abolished slavery 'except as a punishment for crime,' and that exception is a door wide enough to march a plantation through. Angola went from plantation to convict-lease camp to prison without ever changing what it did with Black bodies and farmland. Three-quarters of the men there will die there. The paperwork is different now. The field is the same field.

n: Parchman Farm — worse than slavery, on purpose
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Mississippi State Penitentiary, Sunflower County, Mississippi Delta)
det: Mississippi Delta, US · Parchman since 1901 — When the South lost its slaves it did not lose its taste for their labour, and in Mississippi the solution was a place called Parchman: eighteen thousand acres of Delta cotton worked from before dawn by Black men who had been swept up under laws written to sweep up Black men, driven by armed convict-guards, and flogged with a leather strap they called Black Annie. A historian titled his book on it 'Worse Than Slavery,' and meant it precisely — a slaveowner had at least a cash reason to keep his property alive, where the state had a fresh supply and no such reason. Parchman is the sound underneath a whole century of the blues; the men sang because singing set the pace of the labour, and because it was the one thing they were allowed to keep. Freedom Riders were sent here in 1961 to be broken. It is still open, still deadly — a federal investigation in our own decade found men dying in filth — a plantation the state of Mississippi has simply never been willing to close.

n: The Alabama coal mines — where 'freed' men were worked to death
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Banner Mine / the Birmingham coal district, Alabama)
ctx: Birmingham district, Alabama, US · convict coal, 1865–1928
det: Birmingham district, Alabama, US · convict coal, 1865–1928 — The cruelest twist of American emancipation was that it came with an exception written into the Constitution — slavery was over, 'except as a punishment for crime' — and the states of the South read that clause like a business plan. They passed laws making it a crime to be Black and idle, arrested men by the thousands on nothing, and leased them to whoever would pay: railroads, brickyards, turpentine camps, and above all the coal mines of Alabama. Here the arithmetic turned even darker than slavery's. A planter who owned a man had a reason, however vile, to keep him alive; a company that merely rented one, with a fresh supply waiting in the county jail, did not. So they worked them until they died, and sent for more. When the Banner Mine near Birmingham exploded in 1911 it killed a hundred and twenty-eight men, and almost every one was a leased convict — Black men sentenced, in effect, to dig coal until the coal or the gas killed them. Alabama did not get around to abolishing this for good until 1928. It has a name, from the book that recovered it: slavery by another name.

n: The fire camps — a dollar an hour to walk into the flames
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (California conservation 'fire' camps; the US prison-labour system)
det: California, US · present (prison fire crews) — When California burns — and in the century this atlas is built for, California burns more every year — some of the people sent to the front line to stop the fire are prisoners, paid about a dollar an hour to swing a tool at a wall of flame. They volunteer, because the fire camps are better than the cells, and because the alternative work pays even less: across the United States incarcerated people earn a few cents an hour, and in a handful of states they are paid nothing at all, because the same constitutional clause that let slavery survive as punishment still does. The men and women in the fire camps do genuinely dangerous, genuinely skilled work; some are hurt, some die; and many, on release, are barred from the very firefighting jobs their prison training qualified them for. In 2024 California put a measure on the ballot to end forced prison labour, and the voters said no. Hold this pin against all the others in this chapter — the trade, the castles, the plantations, the leased men in the mines. This is the same river, still running, in the present tense: a dollar an hour, into the flames, in the richest state of the richest country on Earth.

n: Kolwezi — the blood in the battery
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Kolwezi / the Copperbelt, Lualaba, DR Congo; + eastern coltan front, Goma/Kivu)
det: Kolwezi, DR Congo · present (cobalt) — Every rechargeable battery in the world — every phone, every laptop, every electric car sold as the clean future — runs on cobalt, and most of the world's cobalt comes out of the ground in one country, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, a great deal of it dug by hand. In the pits around Kolwezi, hundreds of thousands of people, including by the American government's own estimate some forty thousand children, claw at the earth with basic tools for a dollar or two a day, breathing cobalt dust, dying in collapses that go unrecorded. A researcher who spent years there concluded flatly that there is no clean cobalt from the Congo — that all of it is touched, somewhere down the chain, by child labour or forced labour or worse — and that if you are reading this on a screen, you are quietly part of the system. Off to the east, around Goma, other minerals for the same devices fund the militias that fight over the mines. This is the pin to sit uneasily beside the castles and the plantations: not a memorial to a finished crime but a live one, in your pocket, right now — the oldest arrangement in this chapter, wearing the newest technology.

n: Libya — the auction blocks came back
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Libya — the coast + migrant detention centres; Tripoli)
det: Libya · since 2017 — In 2017 a television crew smuggled a camera into a field outside Tripoli and filmed men being sold — a real auction, a man going for the equivalent of four hundred dollars, 'big strong boys for farm work' — and the world was briefly shocked to learn that in the wreckage of Libya the slave market had come back, not as metaphor but as an actual event with a starting price. It has not stopped. Migrants trying to reach Europe are caught and held in detention centres run by militias, where they are beaten, starved, sold, and tortured while their families are made to listen on the phone until they pay a ransom. And here is the part that implicates more than the traffickers: the European Union, to keep these people off its shores, pays and equips the Libyan coast guard to catch them at sea and deliver them back into exactly these centres. The auction block did not survive because the world forgot slavery was wrong. It came back because a border was worth more to someone than the people piling up against it.

n: Xinjiang — the state as the trafficker
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region, NW China; Urumqi)
det: Xinjiang, NW China · since 2017 — The largest system of forced labour on Earth right now belongs not to a warlord or a cartel but to a government. Since 2017 China has put somewhere between one and two million Uyghurs and other Muslims through a network of internment camps in its far west — for the crime of their faith and ethnicity — and has built into it a machine for extracting their work: sewing, picking, assembling, first in the camps and then in factories across the country to which they are 'transferred' under guard. The output is not obscure. Xinjiang grows a fifth of the world's cotton and makes nearly half its solar-grade silicon, which means a genocide is stitched into a great deal of the world's clothing and bolted onto a great many of its solar panels — the panels sold, again, as the clean future. When the trafficker is the state itself, there is no owner to prosecute and no border to cross to escape; the accountability runs to a government, and to every supply chain that finds it cheaper not to look. The camps are real. The cotton is in the shirt. The atlas declines to pretend it does not know.

n: Mauritania — where you can still be born property
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Islamic Republic of Mauritania; Nouakchott)
det: Mauritania · criminalized 2007/2015; still practised — In Mauritania a person can still be born a slave — not metaphorically, not in the sense of a bad job, but owned, the inherited property of another family, unpaid, and unfree to leave, with children who are born owned in turn. It is the last country on Earth to abolish slavery, which it did on paper in 1981 and did not make an actual crime until 2007, and even now the arrangement runs quietly through a racial caste system centuries old: the lighter-skinned Bidhan at the top, the darker Haratin as the hereditary property at the bottom. What makes Mauritania a cursed place and not merely a poor one is the state's response, which is to insist the slavery does not exist, to almost never prosecute an owner — and to put the people who campaign against it in prison instead. When a country jails the abolitionists faster than the slaveholders, it has told you which side it is on. The chains here were never a memorial. They were never taken off.

n: The kafala Gulf — the World Cup that men died to build
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Qatar / the Gulf kafala states; Doha)
det: Qatar & the Gulf · kafala; 2010–2022 — The 2022 World Cup was played in air-conditioned stadiums that migrant workers died building, in a country whose entire labour system is a soft form of ownership. Under the Gulf's kafala rules a worker's right to be in the country belongs to his employer: the employer takes the passport, controls whether the man can change jobs or go home, and often simply does not pay — an arrangement the workers, mostly from South Asia and Africa, describe in the language of bondage. To build Qatar's tournament, some two million of them laid the roads and raised the towers in desert heat, and a newspaper counted at least sixty-five hundred deaths of workers from five South Asian countries in the decade after Qatar won the bid — many written off as 'natural,' young men's hearts stopping in the night. Football's governing body handed Qatar the tournament and staged it anyway, and the world watched, which is the accountability trail arriving, as it so often does in this chapter, at all of us in the stands. The stadiums are beautiful. Ask what they are made of.

n: CoreCivic & GEO Group — the companies that need you locked up
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (CoreCivic HQ, Brentwood/Nashville, Tennessee; GEO Group HQ, Boca Raton, Florida)
det: US (HQ Tennessee & Florida) · since 1983 — Most people locked in a private prison or an immigration jail in the United States are held by one of two companies — CoreCivic and the GEO Group — and it is worth being precise about what that means: these are publicly traded corporations whose revenue depends on the number of human beings held in cages, and who have built their business so that the number can only be kept high. Roughly three-quarters of their contracts include a clause guaranteeing a minimum occupancy, which means the taxpayer pays for the empty beds too, which means there is a standing financial reason to fill them. Immigration detention pays them by the head, by the day. And they spend accordingly: millions a year lobbying, half a million dollars each to a single presidential inauguration, quiet work through the bodies that draft state laws. When a company's earnings rise every time a judge or an agent decides to detain someone, the incentive has been placed exactly where it should never be. This pin is not on the prison. It is on the boardroom in Tennessee, and the one in Florida, where the decision to make the caging of people a growth industry is actually taken.

n: Dilley — the profit in caging children
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (South Texas Family Residential Center, Dilley, Texas; operated by CoreCivic for ICE)
det: Dilley, Texas, US · since c. 2014 — There is a place in south Texas called Dilley where the United States pays a private company roughly a hundred and eighty million dollars a year to hold migrant parents and their children behind a fence, and where — according to filings by children's rights lawyers — the children have been denied medical care, clean drinking water, food a child can eat, and enough sleep, in a facility that recently reported an outbreak of measles. The company is paid by the head and by the day, which is the whole problem in a sentence: every additional child, and every additional night, is revenue. The center was closed, then reopened when the politics changed, because the demand for it is manufactured — Congress has at times simply written a minimum number of detention beds into law, a quota the industry lobbied to create, so the beds must be filled whether or not anyone needs filling. Keep this pin in view as the century warms and the number of people fleeing uninhabitable places climbs: the machinery for detaining them at a profit is already built, already tested, already sending an invoice.

n: The phone in the cage — Securus, ViaPath & the billionaire's cut
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Platinum Equity HQ, Beverly Hills, California — owner of Securus/Aventiv)
det: US · present — If you are poor and someone you love is in prison, the phone call costs you, and for years a fifteen-minute call could cost more than eight dollars, a video visit fifteen, an email fifty cents — set by one of two companies that between them wire nearly every prison and jail in America. The mechanism is elegant and vile: the jail awards the contract to whichever firm offers it the biggest kickback, so the firm jacks up the rate to cover the kickback, so the poorest families in the country end up subsidizing the sheriff's budget every time they say goodnight to a father. The larger of these firms, Securus, is owned by the private-equity house of a billionaire who also owns an NBA team; he bought it with a mountain of borrowed money and has squeezed it to service the debt. A grandmother named Martha Wright-Reed spent decades fighting to make these calls cheap enough to reach her grandson, and in 2023 a law was finally named for her, and the regulators capped the rates — and then, in 2026, the regulators quietly gutted the caps again. Follow the accountability and it ends, as it so often does, in a very nice office in Beverly Hills.

n: The Texas Two-Step — how a prison-health firm made its dead into creditors
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (staged via Texas reincorporation + a Houston bankruptcy; Corizon origin, Brentwood, Tennessee)
det: US · 2022–2023 — A company was paid to keep prisoners alive and healthy, and by the account of thousands of lawsuits it did the opposite — people died and were maimed for want of basic care — and when the bills for that came due, the company performed a maneuver that has a name in the trade: the Texas Two-Step. It re-incorporated itself into Texas, split into two, put all its money and contracts in one half and all its dead and injured and their claims in the other, and then sent the second half into bankruptcy. The victims woke up transformed from plaintiffs into unsecured creditors, standing in line for cents on the dollar behind everyone else. A former executive called it plainly what it was — a scheme to take the assets and abandon the liabilities — and a director of the discarded shell admitted the point was to force people into settling for less. Two U.S. senators and a federal watchdog objected; the maneuver mostly worked anyway. It is the purest expression of the whole business: privatize the profit from neglecting a prisoner, and when the neglect kills him, declare the debt to his mother bankrupt.

n: Kids for cash — Luzerne County, where children were sold by the bench
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Luzerne County / Wilkes-Barre, Pennsylvania; PA Child Care + Western PA Child Care)
det: Luzerne County, Pennsylvania, US · 2003–2008 (exposed 2009) — In one Pennsylvania county, for five years, children were quite literally sold by the judge. Mark Ciavarella, the juvenile court judge, took millions in kickbacks — the total came to about two point eight million dollars — from the men who built and co-owned two new for-profit juvenile jails, while his colleague on the bench shut down the county's own center to guarantee the private ones stayed full. Then Ciavarella filled them: children as young as eight and ten, hauled up on things like petty theft or a mocking post about a school official, sentenced in hearings that lasted minutes, often with no lawyer, shackled on the spot and driven away without being allowed to hug their parents goodbye. Thousands of these convictions were later thrown out and expunged; the judges went to federal prison; a court ordered them to pay two hundred and six million dollars they will never have. But the arithmetic underneath is the thing this atlas records: a child, converted into a per-night payment, is worth more to certain people locked up than free. One of those children grew up and did not survive it, and his mother said so to the judge's face on the courthouse steps. This is where the profit motive and the cell meet with nothing in between — not even the pretense of justice.

n: G4S & Serco — billing the government to tag the dead
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (UK Ministry of Justice / Serious Fraud Office matter; G4S + Serco, London)
det: UK · tagging scandal, 2013 — The most efficient way to understand the outsourcing of punishment is this: two of the world's largest security companies spent years billing the British government to electronically tag criminals, and among the criminals they were charging to monitor were people who had gone back to prison, people who had left the country, people whose tags had been cut off long ago, people who had never been tagged at all — and people who were dead. The overcharging ran to tens of millions of pounds and went on for the better part of a decade before anyone in the Ministry noticed, or admitted noticing. When it finally surfaced, one firm paid a multi-million-pound fraud penalty and the other repaid what it had taken, and the contracts rolled on to the next company in the queue, because the model itself was never really in question. There is a special clarity to a company invoicing a government for the surveillance of a corpse. It tells you that once the caging and watching of human beings becomes a line of business, the humans stop being the point — the invoice is the point — and the ankle on which the tag is meant to sit need not even be attached to anyone alive.

n: Nauru & Manus — the most expensive cages on Earth
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Nauru + Manus Island, PNG; Australian offshore detention regime)
det: Nauru & Manus (PNG) · offshore detention, 2012– — Australia built the most expensive cages on Earth, and then hired companies to run them. To deter people from arriving by boat, it ships asylum seekers to two remote islands — Nauru and Manus — and swears none will ever be allowed to settle in Australia, and pays private contractors sums that beggar belief to keep them there: at the peak, something like four million dollars per person per year, eleven thousand dollars a day, to hold human beings in conditions that leaked internal reports describe in the language of systemic abuse — of adults and of children — of medical neglect, of self-harm, of a young Iranian man named Reza Barati beaten to death in a riot in a camp a British security firm was paid to run. The cost per head actually rose as the number held fell, because the contractors were paid to be there regardless, and the profit margins written into the deals ran to twenty and thirty percent. The government settled the detainees' lawsuit for seventy million dollars while admitting nothing. This is the pin the atlas most wants you to carry forward: as the warming world pushes more people off their land and toward richer shores, this — the remote island, the private operator, the astronomical invoice, the deniable cruelty — is the model already sitting on the shelf, tested and ready.

n: Dahlonega — the gold that scattered the profit on purpose
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Dahlonega, Lumpkin County, Georgia; Cherokee country)
det: Dahlonega, Georgia, US · gold rush, 1829– — The gold came first. In 1829 it was found in the north Georgia hills, on Cherokee land, and at the peak the mines gave up something like three hundred ounces a day — enough that the speculators stopped pretending and demanded the state simply take the country from the people who lived on it. They got their wish; the Removal Act followed within the year. But watch what Georgia did next, because it is the whole reason this crime is so hard to bill to anyone. It did not hand the Cherokee homeland to a company. It ran a lottery. The land was cut into eighteen thousand lots and drawn like a raffle by eighty-five thousand ordinary Georgians for the price of a few dollars a ticket, and as each winner was named, the Cherokee family living on that lot was told to leave. The theft was deliberately atomised — spread so thin across so many small winners that no single hand is dirty enough to point at. That is most of the answer to who profited from the Trail of Tears: almost everyone, a little, on purpose. A federal mint opened here to stamp the gold into coin. The profit was designed to disappear into the crowd, and it did.

n: The Hermitage — the man who signed it, and cashed in
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (The Hermitage, Davidson County, near Nashville, Tennessee)
ctx: near Nashville, Tennessee, US · Jackson; Indian Removal Act 1830
det: near Nashville, Tennessee, US · Jackson; Indian Removal Act 1830 — If you want one name on the Trail of Tears, it is the man who lived here. Andrew Jackson wrote the Indian Removal Act, drove it through Congress, and enforced it — and he was not a disinterested statesman doing a grim duty. He was a cotton planter who enslaved some hundred and fifty people on this ground, a land speculator by the historians' own plain description, and a general who had spent years taking Native land by treaty and then buying into the country he had just opened. He once stripped twenty-three million acres from the Creek in a single treaty, including from Creek who had fought at his side. The Hermitage is where the architect of removal grew his cotton and counted his acres, and it is the closest this whole scattered crime comes to a boardroom: the house of the man who both signed the policy and stood to gain from it. He is on the twenty-dollar bill. The people he removed are on a trail named for their weeping.

n: New Echota — the treaty ninety percent of them never signed
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (New Echota, near Calhoun, Gordon County, Georgia)
ctx: New Echota, Georgia, US · Treaty 1835; removal 1838
det: New Echota, Georgia, US · Treaty 1835; removal 1838 — Every forced removal needs a piece of paper, and the Trail of Tears has one, signed right here in what was then the Cherokee capital. The catch is that the men who signed it had no right to. The Treaty of New Echota was put through by a faction of perhaps a hundred people — persuaded that removal was inevitable and better bargained for than resisted — while the elected Cherokee government under John Ross, and something like nine in ten of the Cherokee people, rejected it as a fraud. The United States Senate ratified it anyway, by a single vote, and then treated it as binding on a whole nation that had never agreed to it. That treaty is the legal machinery of the Trail; without it the roundups two years later would have had no cover. The men who signed were later killed by their own people under a law against selling the national land. And this same town housed the Cherokee Phoenix, the first newspaper printed in a Native language — a nation demonstrating, in its own alphabet, exactly the civilisation it was about to be told it lacked.

n: Fort Cass — where the removal was a business paid by the head
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Fort Cass, Charleston, Bradley County, Tennessee; on the Hiwassee)
det: Charleston, Tennessee, US · removal, 1838 — The removal was not only a policy; it was a contract, and Fort Cass is where you can see the invoice. This was the headquarters — the general's tent, the largest of the stockades, where perhaps sixteen thousand Cherokee were penned through a Tennessee summer and hundreds died of dysentery in the heat before a single one had started west. And the machine that moved them ran on money. The men who supplied the removal were paid by the head, which gave them every reason to pack the boats and wagons past safety; the men who fed it billed spoiled rations at inflated prices. Somebody profited from every mile and every meal. But here the accountability trail goes deliberately faint, and honesty requires saying so: there is no single fat contractor to name, because the graft was spread across scattered suppliers and quartermasters — and, the bitterest turn of all, the United States eventually made the Cherokee Nation organise and pay for its own deportation, reimbursed by the head. They were charged, in the end, for the trail that killed them.

n: Chapel Hill — the endowment that still earns off the taking
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (University of North Carolina, Chapel Hill; + the national Morrill Act machine)
ctx: UNC, North Carolina, US · Morrill Act, 1862
det: UNC, North Carolina, US · Morrill Act, 1862 — Everywhere else in this chapter the money vanished into the crowd — the lottery winners, the cotton economy, the contractors no one can name. Here it did not. American universities keep careful books, and the books show it. The University of North Carolina was built, from its founding, on more than two hundred thousand acres of Cherokee and Chickasaw land, and it is thriving still. It is one thread of a much larger seam: in 1862 the Morrill Act converted almost eleven million acres taken from two hundred and fifty tribes into the seed endowments of fifty-two universities — for which the government paid, in total, less than four hundred thousand dollars, and often nothing at all. And because the law required that the principal be kept forever, that money is still there, still earning, on the ledgers of schools your children may attend. Honesty demands the caveat: most of that Morrill land was taken later and further west — from the Dakota, from the Cheyenne a year after the Sand Creek massacre, from the Navajo after the Long Walk — not on the Trail itself. But it is the same machine, and it is the one place the dispossession is still a line item you can read. Everywhere else they scattered the profit so you couldn't find it. The universities wrote it down.

n: Carlisle — 'kill the Indian, save the man'
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Carlisle, Cumberland County, Pennsylvania; now the US Army War College)
det: Carlisle, Pennsylvania, US · 1879–1918 — The last of the Indian wars was fought in a classroom, and it began here. In 1879 an army officer named Richard Henry Pratt, who had spent his career fighting Native nations and then jailing their people, persuaded the government that it would be cheaper to erase the next generation than to keep killing this one. His slogan was 'kill the Indian... save the man,' and he meant the first half nearly literally: take the children — some as young as six — strip them of their language, their names, their hair, their clothes, and their gods, and hand back something that would answer to English. Carlisle took in something like ten thousand children from nearly every nation in the country, and it worked well enough to become the template — copied into some three hundred and fifty schools across the United States, and exported north to build Canada's. Not all the children survived the experiment; at least a hundred and ninety-two are buried in the school's own cemetery, and only in the last few years have tribes begun bringing them home. The place where the policy was born is now the college where the United States Army teaches strategy.

n: The Interior Department — the office that ran the schools, and finally counted the graves
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (US Department of the Interior, Washington DC; the national boarding-school system)
det: Washington, D.C., US · boarding schools, 1819–1969 — For a hundred and fifty years the United States took Native children from their families — tens of thousands of them, some only four years old — and sent them to schools designed to make them stop being Native: four hundred and seventeen of them, run in partnership with the churches, paid for out of the federal treasury. Children who broke the rules were locked in solitary, flogged, starved, made to beat each other. And then, for most of a century, almost no one counted the dead. The counting finally happened in our own decade, and it happened from inside the very building responsible: the Department of the Interior, which had run the schools, was by then led by Deb Haaland, the first Native person to sit in the American cabinet, and herself the descendant of children who had been taken. Her investigation confirmed at least nine hundred and seventy-three dead children in seventy-four burial grounds, and said plainly that the real number is far higher. A president apologised for the first time in 2024. The apology is the ledger's last line; the graves are still being found.

n: Kamloops — the radar over the residential schools
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Kamloops Indian Residential School, British Columbia; the Canadian residential system)
ctx: Kamloops, British Columbia, Canada · school 1890–1978; graves 2021
det: Kamloops, British Columbia, Canada · school 1890–1978; graves 2021 — In the spring of 2021 a First Nation in British Columbia pointed ground-penetrating radar at the field beside a former residential school in Kamloops and found what looked like two hundred and fifteen unmarked graves — and Canada, which had been told this for decades and not listened, finally listened. The residential schools ran from the 1830s to 1996; a hundred and fifty thousand Indigenous children passed through some hundred and thirty-nine of them, run by the churches on the government's behalf, and the country's own Truth and Reconciliation Commission called what happened there a cultural genocide and named more than four thousand children who died in them — a figure its chairman thought closer to six. Honesty requires a careful line here, and the atlas draws it: the deaths are documented beyond dispute; the radar 'graves,' the two hundred at Kamloops and the seven hundred and fifty-one at Marieval, are probable burials not yet dug up — which is precisely what makes the churches' long refusal to open their records so damning, because they could end the doubt and have chosen not to. Ottawa apologised and paid billions. The Catholic Church, which ran most of the schools, held out until 2022, when the Pope came to Canadian soil to say the word: genocide.

n: Altiplano — the kingpin who bought his way out, twice
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Altiplano / CEFERESO No. 1, Almoloya de Juarez, Mexico state; + Puente Grande, Jalisco)
ctx: Almoloya de Juárez, Mexico · El Chapo escapes, 2001 & 2015
det: Almoloya de Juárez, Mexico · El Chapo escapes, 2001 & 2015 — Twice, Mexico put its most powerful drug lord in its most secure prison, and twice he simply left. The first time, from Puente Grande in 2001, the official story was a laundry cart, though people who worked there say he walked out the front in a guard's uniform with paid-off officers holding the doors. The second time, from Altiplano in 2015 — the prison the government swore was escape-proof — his people dug a mile-long tunnel, ventilated and lit, with a small motorcycle on rails to carry him, that surfaced precisely under the one blind spot in his cell, the shower. Inside, for years, he had lived the way cartel money lets a man live anywhere: women, feasts, contraband, a staff of the bribed. Every wall of every maximum-security prison in Mexico turned out to have a price. There is a coda the atlas keeps: extradited to the United States, El Chapo landed in ADX Florence in Colorado, the federal supermax where the cells have no tunnels to dig under and no guards who can be bought — and from which his own lawyers now complain, as the powerful always eventually do, that the conditions are inhuman.

n: Bilibid — the drug lords' jacuzzis, and the war they were used to justify
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (New Bilibid Prison, Muntinlupa, Metro Manila, Philippines)
ctx: Muntinlupa, Metro Manila, Philippines · drug war, c. 2014–2016
det: Muntinlupa, Metro Manila, Philippines · drug war, c. 2014–2016 — When police raided the maximum-security wing of Manila's Bilibid prison in 2014, they found the convicted drug lords living in what the reporters could only call villas: air conditioning, jacuzzis, a sauna, a recording studio with a stage, a bar, a sex doll, wristwatches worth more than a guard earned in a decade — and the phones from which they were still running the drug trade across the country, because a bribed guard is cheaper than a wall. The luxury itself is an old story; corruption buys comfort everywhere money is strong and the state is weak. What makes Bilibid cursed is what was built on top of it. The scandal became the emblem a politician named Rodrigo Duterte used to justify a 'drug war' that killed some twenty-three thousand people, most of them poor, many simply shot in the street — and then he turned the same weapon on the woman who had led the raids, jailing Senator Leila de Lima for years on drug charges that collapsed when the witnesses recanted. The jacuzzis were real. So was everything the outrage over them was used to excuse. Follow the accountability past the marble.

n: Landsberg — the comfortable cell where Mein Kampf was written
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Landsberg am Lech, Bavaria, Germany)
ctx: Landsberg am Lech, Bavaria, Germany · Hitler jailed 1924
det: Landsberg am Lech, Bavaria, Germany · Hitler jailed 1924 — The most consequential act of leniency in modern history happened in a comfortable cell in Bavaria. After his first attempt to seize Germany failed in 1923, Adolf Hitler was convicted of treason and sentenced to five years — and served nine months, in a fortress-confinement wing that spared him labour, censorship, and hardship, where he received a stream of admiring visitors, ate well enough to put on weight, and treated his cell as a private study. He used the time to dictate the first volume of the book that would name the coming genocide in advance, and to gather the men who would carry it out. A court had the architect of the catastrophe in its hands, called him a traitor, and gave him a desk and a view. The prison closed the circle itself: two decades later the United States Army took it over and hanged more than two hundred and fifty Nazi war criminals in its yard — among them the men this prison's most famous inmate had made. Comfort for one monster in 1924; the gallows for his students by 1951. The atlas keeps it as a warning about the cost of treating a dangerous man as a harmless one.

n: Club Fed — the softest cages, reserved for the white collar
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Federal Prison Camp, Pensacola, Florida; the US minimum-security camp system)
det: US minimum-security camps · present — The tennis courts are gone, and the golf course was always a myth — the only inmates who touch it are the ones sent to mow it. So set aside the folklore of Club Fed, because the real thing it names is worse and truer than a swimming pool. American justice sorts its prisoners by class before it sorts them by crime. The man who steals a car goes behind razor wire, among the fences and the violence; the man who steals a pension fund goes to a fenceless camp with a dormitory and a lawn, because his crime was committed in a suit. And inside any prison, from the softest camp to the hardest pen, money still works: it buys commissary food, phone time, a lawyer who wins the better placement, even a consultant — there is a whole industry of them — who coaches the wealthy on how to serve the gentlest possible version of a sentence. That is the corruption worth pinning here, and it needs no jacuzzi. It is the quiet, legal, everyday machinery by which a country enslaves its poor in its prisons and pampers its rich in the same system, and calls the whole thing equal justice under law.

n: Moore River — the settlement at the end of the rabbit-proof fence
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Moore River Native Settlement, Mogumber, Western Australia; the Stolen Generations)
det: Mogumber, Western Australia · Stolen Generations, 1918–1951 — For most of the twentieth century Australia took Aboriginal children from their mothers on the theory that the race was dying and the children would be better off white — somewhere between one in ten and one in three of them, a whole people's childhood, removed to missions and settlements and the homes of strangers, and forbidden to speak the languages that would have told them who they were. One of the places they were sent, Moore River in Western Australia, is the settlement three girls escaped in 1931, walking a thousand miles home along the rabbit-proof fence that ran the length of the continent — a true story, and the exception that proves how rarely the taken came home. Australia's own inquiry, in 1997, read the record — the systematic removal of a people's children specifically to erase them as a people — and found it met the definition of genocide. It took another eleven years for a prime minister to say sorry, and his predecessor had refused even that, for fear the word would cost money. The nation apologised. It declined to pay. The atlas notes which of those two came easily.

n: Wellington — the schools that beat out the language, and the reckoning that named it
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Wellington / NZ Parliament; the Native Schools + state-care system)
det: New Zealand · Native Schools, 1867–1969 — New Zealand did to the Maori language what all these schools did to their children's tongues: it beat it out of them. The Native Schools, which ran for a century, taught in English only and punished children for speaking te reo Maori, and they did their work so well that the language nearly died and had to be brought back from the edge a generation later. That was the assimilation. The reckoning came in our own decade, and it was vast: a Royal Commission, the largest inquiry the country has ever held, found that some two hundred thousand people had been abused in state and church care over seventy years — close to one in three of everyone who passed through it — with Maori children, and disabled children, taken first and hurt most, some tortured with electric shocks in a state hospital. The commission called it an unthinkable national catastrophe, and in 2024 the prime minister stood in Parliament and apologised, and asked the Pope and the Archbishop of Canterbury to do the same. The same machine ran in Washington, in Ottawa, in Canberra, and here at the bottom of the world: take the children, forbid the language, and call it care. Four countries, one blueprint.

n: Guantánamo — the island built to be outside the law
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (US Naval Base, Guantánamo Bay, Cuba)
det: Guantánamo Bay, Cuba · since 2002 — The United States built a prison on the one patch of ground it controlled but did not legally own — a naval base on Cuban soil, leased since 1903 — for the express purpose of putting the men it caught after September 11th somewhere its own courts could not reach. That was the design, stated plainly by the lawyers who drew it up: an island outside the law, where a person could be held without charge and questioned without limit. Some eight hundred men passed through it. Only a handful were ever charged with anything; many were tortured, some first in the CIA's secret prisons and then here; a few were boys; a number turned out to be innocent men sold for bounties. Two decades on, around fifteen remain, most never charged and never tried, a category the guards themselves call the forever prisoners. Nine died inside without ever seeing a courtroom. Two presidents promised to close it and did not. And no one — not an interrogator, not a lawyer, not an official — has ever been punished for any of it. That is the part the atlas underlines: not only that a democracy built such a place, but that it faced no consequence for doing so.

n: Abu Ghraib — the photographs, and the system behind them
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Abu Ghraib prison, west of Baghdad, Iraq; + the global CIA black-site network)
det: west of Baghdad, Iraq · 2003–2004 — The photographs are the thing everyone remembers — the hood, the wires, the human pyramid, the American soldiers grinning and giving a thumbs-up over the bodies — and the government's first move was to call it the work of a few bad apples on a night shift. It was not. The techniques in those pictures were a program: designed at Guantánamo, blessed in secret legal memos that redefined torture until almost nothing counted, and run through a network of hidden CIA prisons scattered across cooperative countries, to which suspects were flown in the night under a bureaucratic euphemism, extraordinary rendition. A Senate report later catalogued what happened in those black sites, and the words in it are not the kind governments usually admit to. A prisoner was tortured to death at Abu Ghraib itself. And when the reckoning came it climbed no higher than the enlisted soldiers in the frame; the men who authored the legal architecture of torture were never charged, and one of them went on to teach law to the young. The atlas keeps Abu Ghraib not for the horror of the images but for the lesson of the aftermath: a state can photograph itself torturing and still find no one at the top to blame.

n: Evin — Iran's university of the disappeared
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Evin Prison, north Tehran, Iran)
det: Tehran, Iran · since 1972; 1988 massacre — Evin sits where the mountains meet the north of Tehran, in one of the city's most beautiful districts, and for half a century it has been the place Iran sends the people it wants silenced — the poets and the professors and the students, so many of them that the prisoners call it a university. Foreigners are held here too, kept as bargaining chips to be traded for money or sanctions relief, a quieter kind of hostage-taking. But the thing that makes Evin more than a harsh prison happened in the summer of 1988, when the state convened secret panels that prisoners came to call death commissions, asked the political inmates a few questions about their beliefs, and hanged those who gave the wrong answers — thousands of them, across the country, over a few months, their bodies tipped into unmarked graves their families are still forbidden to mourn at. One of the men who sent them to the rope later became president. No one has answered for it. The prison is still working, still full, still doing quietly what it has always done.

n: Perm-36 — the last Gulag camp, and the fight to forget it
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Perm-36 / Kuchino, Perm Krai, Russia; east of the Urals)
ctx: Perm Krai, Russia · Gulag to 1987; museum fight, 2010s
det: Perm Krai, Russia · Gulag to 1987; museum fight, 2010s — Somewhere between twelve and eighteen million people passed through the Soviet Gulag, and millions never came out, and of the twelve thousand camps that held them exactly one survives — a cluster of wooden barracks in the forest east of the Urals called Perm-36, the last place the USSR kept its dissidents locked away, the poets and translators and rights campaigners it could not otherwise silence. After the Soviet collapse a handful of local historians turned it into a museum, the only one of its kind in the country, a place where Russians could stand inside the machine and reckon with what it had done. And then, as a new Russian state rediscovered its taste for strength and for Stalin, it took the museum back: cut the funding, won the lawsuit, evicted the historians, and quietly rewrote the exhibits until the words political repression fell away and the prisoners became, once again, traitors. The camp is the rarest kind of ruin — a crime with the evidence still standing — and the struggle over it is the whole argument in miniature: not only what a country did, but whether it will be allowed to remember it.

n: Sednaya — the slaughterhouse, when the doors finally opened
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Sednaya/Saydnaya Military Prison, ~30 km N of Damascus, Syria)
det: near Damascus, Syria · to 2024 (regime fall) — For decades Syrians said the name Sednaya only in a whisper. It was the prison north of Damascus where the Assad family sent those it wanted to erase, and what happened inside was so total that a human-rights report could only call it a human slaughterhouse: thousands of people taken from their cells in the middle of the night, blindfolded, told nothing, and hanged in batches, week after week, for years, their bodies trucked to mass graves outside the city. Then, on a December night in 2024, the regime fell — the dictator's plane lifted off for Moscow — and the doors of Sednaya were broken open at last. Thousands walked out. But the crowds that poured up the mountain road, holding phones with photographs of missing sons and brothers, mostly did not find them; after the first day almost no one alive was found, because almost no one had been left alive. A hundred and fifty thousand Syrians had disappeared into that system, and Sednaya was where the disappearing ended. The atlas records it at the moment the doors opened — the rarest thing in this whole grim chapter: a prison of the disappeared, finally emptied, with the world allowed at last to look inside.

n: The goitre belts — where the soil has no iodine
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (the Alpine goitre belt, Switzerland; + the Himalayas, Andes, inland Africa)
det: Alps, Himalayas, Andes · pre-iodization (before 1920s) — There are belts of the Earth — the high inland places, the Alps and the Himalayas and the Andes, the leached soils far from any sea — where the ground itself lacks a single trace element, iodine, and where for that reason alone whole populations once grew the swollen necks called goitre, and their children were born with a damage to the brain and body that no later care could undo. It was never a disease of dirt or bad living; it was geology, the plain absence of one element from the local food. And it has the simplest fix in all of medicine: a pinch of iodine in the salt. Switzerland has added it, without a break, since 1922, and the affliction that shadowed its valleys for centuries vanished in a generation. The atlas flags this not as history but as a standing hazard for anyone settling the high country in a world that can no longer ship a bag of iodised salt over the mountains. The sea feeds the thyroid from a great distance; sever that thread, eat only what the local soil grows, and the swellings and the harmed infants will come back — quietly, a generation deep, before anyone thinks to blame the ground.

n: Pellagra — the four D's, and the tyranny of corn
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (the US South / Spartanburg SC; the corn-monoculture foodshed)
det: US South (Spartanburg, SC) · c. 1906–1940s — The corn will keep you alive and then it will take your mind. For most of a century the American South learned this the hard way: its poor, kept on the thinnest of diets by the cotton economy — cornbread, a little pork fat, molasses — came down in their hundreds of thousands with a disease that ran a grim four-act course: dermatitis, then diarrhoea, then dementia, then death. It was pellagra, and it was nothing but the lack of a single vitamin that corn will not give up unless you treat it with lime first — the way the peoples of Mexico had known to do for millennia, and the South had never learned. When a government doctor proved it was the diet and not a germ, the region fought him, because the truth was an accusation: the disease was made of poverty. The atlas keeps pellagra as the clearest warning of the single-crop foodshed. A staple can feed a people and still be missing the one thing they die without, and the knowledge of how to fix it — a handful of lime, a little variety — is exactly the kind of knowledge a narrowed, hungry world forgets first.

n: Beriberi — the polished-rice disease
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Java / Batavia, Dutch East Indies; the rice-eating world)
det: Java, Dutch East Indies · 1880s–1900s — The prettier rice is the one that kills you. When machines began, in the late nineteenth century, to polish rice white — grinding away the brown bran to make a grain that looked cleaner and kept longer — a strange paralysis and heart failure spread through the rice-eating world, worst among exactly those who could afford the fine milled rice: soldiers, sailors, prisoners, the well-fed. It was beriberi, and it was the bran they had thrown away, which held the one vitamin the white kernel lacked. A doctor in Java noticed his laboratory hens falling ill on polished rice and recovering on the discarded husks, and pulled from that thread the whole idea of the vitamin. A navy that varied its sailors' food escaped it; an army that clung to pure white rice buried tens of thousands of its own men from a disease no enemy inflicted. The atlas sets beriberi beside pellagra as the twin warning of the refined staple: the instinct to make food whiter, smoother, and more durable is precisely the instinct that strips out what the body cannot live without.

n: St. Anthony's Fire — the fungus in the rye
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Saint-Antoine-l'Abbaye, Dauphiné, France; the rye lands of Europe)
ctx: rye lands of Europe · medieval; 944 CE & 1692
det: rye lands of Europe · medieval; 944 CE & 1692 — In a bad wet year, in a country that lived on rye, the bread itself could turn on you. A fungus called ergot grows in place of the grain, hard and black and easy to miss once it is milled into the flour, and the poison it carries does one of two terrible things: it clamps the blood vessels shut until the fingers and toes and then the limbs blacken and rot away, a burning agony the medieval world called St. Anthony's Fire — or it seizes the brain, and a village convulses and hallucinates and dances itself to exhaustion. Whole regions were struck at once; one tenth-century French outbreak is said to have killed forty thousand. Some historians think a rye harvest gone to ergot is what set the girls of Salem screaming. The atlas keeps this as the warning of the stored crop: a single grain, grown wet and inspected poorly, can poison everyone who shares the loaf — and a warming world that delivers damp summers to the rye lands is a world that invites the fire back. Know your fungus. Watch the black kernels. Do not eat the bread of a rotten year.

n: The grass pea — the famine food that cripples
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (the Ethiopian highlands; + central India, Bangladesh — the grass-pea lands)
ctx: Ethiopian highlands; India; Bangladesh · recurrent famine years
det: Ethiopian highlands; India; Bangladesh · recurrent famine years — There is a crop that will grow when nothing else will — through drought, through flood, on ruined ground, asking almost nothing — and in a failing world that makes it precious beyond price and dangerous beyond reckoning. The grass pea feeds people when the harvest dies, which is why it is a famine food; but it carries a slow neurotoxin, and a person who eats little else for a few months, as one does in a famine, can be left with the legs paralysed for the rest of their life. It takes the young men first. It has crippled the poor of the Ethiopian highlands and central India and Bengal in their famine years, and Goya drew its victims in Spain. The cruelty of it is exact: the very crop that carries a people through the hungry season is the one that, leaned on too hard, takes their walking with it. The atlas marks the grass pea as the emblematic hazard of the 2060 foodshed — because in a hotter, harder century the drought-proof crop is the one everyone will reach for, and the settlement that lives on it alone will save its bodies and lose its legs. Soak it, boil it, and never let it be the only thing on the table.

n: The town-killing train — hazmat on aging rail
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (East Palestine, Ohio; + Lac-Mégantic QC, Graniteville SC)
det: East Palestine, Ohio, US · 2023 (& Lac-Mégantic 2013) — A mile of freight cars, some of them full of chemicals that turn to poison gas, rolls through the middle of a sleeping town on rails and wheels that no one has quite kept up with — and one night the cars leave the track. In East Palestine, Ohio, in 2023, the answer to a pile-up of ruptured tankers was to set fire to four hundred tons of vinyl chloride on purpose, sending a black cloud over the county and leaving the townspeople to wonder for years afterward what it had done to their water and their children. It was not a freak event. A runaway oil train had already erased the centre of Lac-Mégantic and forty-seven of its people; a split chlorine car had already killed nine in Graniteville. The trains only get longer, the inspections thinner, the cargo no less toxic. The atlas files the hazmat railway as a latent hazard because a world that gives up trucks and planes will lean on rail more than ever, inheriting a network built long ago and maintained on the cheap — and every town the line runs through is downwind of the next derailment. Where the rails pass the houses, read this pin twice.

n: The bridges past their span
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Morandi Bridge, Genoa, Italy; + I-35W Minneapolis, Fern Hollow Pittsburgh)
det: Genoa (Morandi) & US · 2018–2022 — A bridge looks like the most solid thing a civilisation makes, and it is in fact one of the most fragile — a machine held up not only by its steel but by the endless, invisible work of inspecting and patching and repainting it, forever. Stop paying that debt and the bridge does not sag politely; it drops. Genoa learned this in 2018, when a fifty-year-old span with corrosion its keepers knew about let go in a rainstorm and took forty-three people down with it. Minneapolis learned it in 2007, when a rush-hour bridge folded into the Mississippi. Pittsburgh woke one morning in 2022 to find a bridge it had rated 'poor' for years simply gone. The engineers who grade such things give the whole aging stock a barely-passing mark and count the deficient spans in the tens of thousands. The atlas keeps this as a warning to the future because a settlement in 2060 will inherit the bridges of the twentieth century without the mills and cranes that raised them — and a crossing you cannot rebuild is a crossing you had better not need. Cross the old bridges lightly, and know which ones you cannot afford to lose.

n: The dams that outlived their keepers
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Derna, Libya; + the global high-hazard dam stock)
det: Derna, Libya (& global) · 2023 — Every dam is a bargain struck with a great weight of water: hold it back, and it gives you drink and power and calm fields; stop maintaining it, and it waits. Derna, on the Libyan coast, kept its bargain badly — two dams from the 1970s, cracked and neglected through years of war and warnings — and when a freak storm filled them past their limit one night in 2023, they burst, and a wall of water swept a quarter of the city and something like fifteen thousand of its people into the sea. It was not bad luck; it was an old machine that no one had paid to keep. The same aging reservoirs stand above thousands of towns; engineers count the dangerous ones in the thousands and cross their fingers each wet spring. The atlas marks the aging dam as a latent hazard of exactly the 2060 kind: a warming climate throws heavier rain at structures built for a gentler one, just as the industry to shore them up falls away. If you settle below a dam, learn its age and its cracks, and know which way is uphill. Water is patient, and the wall is only as strong as the last repair.

n: The levee, and the false safety of the wall
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (New Orleans, USA; + every leveed + walled coast)
det: New Orleans, US · 2005 (Katrina) — A sea wall is a promise, and the danger of a promise is that people believe it. New Orleans believed its levees, and built its neighbourhoods low and dense behind them because the wall said the water would not come; and in 2005 the wall failed — not overtopped by an unimaginable storm but broken at its foundations, in some fifty places, by flaws its own engineers had built in — and four-fifths of the city went under, and eighteen hundred people died. The cruelty of the engineered defence is precisely that it works, for a while: it hides the risk instead of removing it, coaxing more people onto the drowned ground the map warns against, until the day it lets go and delivers the whole flood at once. The atlas underlines this because it is the atlas's own future in miniature. A world of collapsed currents and risen seas will crowd behind levees + barriers it can barely maintain, trusting walls that fail suddenly + completely. Read this pin beside the submergence layer. A wall is not dry land. It is a bet, and the house does not always pay.

n: Uruk — the field the state learned to count
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Uruk / Warka, southern Iraq)
ctx: Uruk, southern Iraq · c. 4th millennium BC
det: Uruk, southern Iraq · c. 4th millennium BC — The first cities kept their records before they kept their poetry, and the first thing anyone wrote down was how much grain was owed. At Uruk, five thousand years ago, four things arrive together — the cereal field, the granary, the tax, and the wedge-shaped script pressed into clay to count it all — and they are not four things but one machine. The reason the state came to rest on grain was never that grain is the finest food; it is that grain is countable. It ripens all at once, stands above the ground where an assessor can see it, and stores in a heap that can be measured and taken. A plot of mixed roots and vines, ripening raggedly underground, feeds a family but cannot be taxed. So the single, standing, countable crop won — not in the kitchen but in the ledger. Every monoculture since is a descendant of that first accountant's convenience: the loop cut not for the harvest's sake but for the record's.

n: Saint-Domingue — where farming was first designed to destroy
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Saint-Domingue / Plaine du Nord, Haiti; + Barbados + Brazil sugar coasts)
det: northern Haiti (Saint-Domingue) · sugar colony to 1791–1804 — Before the plantation, farming took from the ground and gave back to it, because the farmer meant to be there the next year. The sugar colonies broke that contract on purpose. Here, on the northern plain of Saint-Domingue and on the coasts of Barbados and Brazil before it, agriculture was redesigned as a machine for turning enslaved lives and living soil into money that sailed away: one crop, no rotation, no return, the ground mined like a seam of coal and abandoned when spent. It was the most profitable thing in the world and the first agriculture built to leave a wasteland — the fertility of the land and the years of half a million stolen people both shipped off as sweetness, neither ever coming back. Every sacrifice zone since, every field farmed to death for a distant market, is working from the template written here. The enslaved of Saint-Domingue eventually burned it down and made a nation; the model, unfortunately, outlived them.

n: Oppau — bread and poison from one bench
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Oppau, Ludwigshafen, Germany)
det: Oppau, Ludwigshafen, Germany · 1913; explosion 1921 — Everything that lets a field forget how to feed itself began at one factory on the Rhine. In 1913 the works at Oppau became the first place on Earth to pull nitrogen out of thin air and fossil energy at industrial scale — the Haber-Bosch process — and with that, the ancient reasons to keep a field diverse simply evaporated. Why rotate legumes, why spread manure, why interplant, when nitrogen now comes in a bag? Half the protein in every living human is built from that air today; so is the runoff choking the world's river-mouths. The man whose name it carries, Fritz Haber, won a Nobel for feeding billions and, in the very same years, personally ran the first chlorine-gas attacks of the Great War — the same mastery of nitrogen chemistry aimed both ways. And the plant kept its own account honest in a terrible fashion: in 1921 its fertiliser store detonated and killed some five hundred and sixty people in an instant. Bread and poison, from one bench, by one hand.

n: The Dust Bowl — when the soil left the continent
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Cimarron County / the southern High Plains, US)
det: southern High Plains, US · 1930s — For ten thousand years the shortgrass prairie held the southern Plains together with roots that went down further than a man stands tall. In twenty years of high wheat prices and new tractors, the settlers tore all of it up and planted a single crop where the sod had been — and when the rains failed in the 1930s and stayed failed, there was nothing left to hold the ground. The soil simply left. It rose in black walls a mile high, buried houses to the eaves, and blew east until it dusted the desks of Washington and settled on ships three hundred miles out in the Atlantic. A quarter of the region's people walked away from land that had turned to sky. The Dust Bowl is the plainest warning the closed loop ever issued: strip the diversity that anchors a place, bet the whole system on the weather staying kind, and one dry decade will call the bluff and carry your continent away on the wind.

n: Punjab — the miracle and its bill, in one landscape
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Punjab, India — Ludhiana)
det: Punjab, India · Green Revolution, 1960s– — Punjab was supposed to be the happy ending — the place where new dwarf wheat and irrigation and fertiliser beat back famine and turned a poor province into a breadbasket, and for a while it was exactly that.
