Read the same fields today and the other column of the ledger has filled in. The water table beneath them is falling by up to half a metre a year, pumped dry to flood a rice crop a near-desert was never meant to grow. The soil is going salty and thin. The insects have outlasted the poisons. And the farmers, made to buy their seed and chemicals fresh each season, have sunk into a debt that has taken thousands of their lives. Nothing here was a lie; the harvests were and are enormous. It is only that the true price was written in a second column nobody read at the time — groundwater, soil, and the people themselves — and the atlas keeps Punjab as the place you can stand and read both columns at once.

n: The Corn Belt and the dead zone — the open loop's exhaust pipe
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Iowa Corn Belt, US — terminus in the Gulf of Mexico)
ctx: Iowa Corn Belt → Gulf of Mexico, US · present
det: Iowa Corn Belt → Gulf of Mexico, US · present — Stand in an Iowa cornfield in July and you are standing at the intake of a pipe that ends eight hundred miles south under the Gulf of Mexico. The corn and soy run to every horizon, fed on nitrogen conjured from fossil gas, and every year the fields take up only some of it; the rest drains into the tile lines, into the creeks, into the Mississippi, and pours out into the sea. There it feeds a bloom of algae that dies and sinks and rots, and the rotting pulls the oxygen from the water until almost nothing can breathe across a stretch of ocean the size of a small state. Fish and shrimp flee it or die in it; the fishermen call it the dead zone. This is what a cut loop looks like drawn at continental scale: the nutrients a healthy soil would cycle back into itself instead travel one way only — out of Iowa, down the river, into a suffocation that returns each summer like a bill. The exhaust pipe of the way we grow food.

n: Levittown — the lawn made compulsory
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Levittown, Long Island, New York)
ctx: Levittown, Long Island, New York, US · 1947
det: Levittown, Long Island, New York, US · 1947 — The lawn used to be a way for the rich to show they could afford to waste good land on grass no sheep or plough would touch. Levittown made it compulsory for everyone. On the potato fields of Long Island in 1947 the first mass-produced American suburb went up — seventeen thousand identical houses — and each came with a patch of lawn that the lease actually required you to mow and forbade you to fence. Overnight the aristocrat's idle green became a moral duty pressed on millions: keep it cut, keep it open, keep it uniform, or answer for it to your neighbours and your covenant. It was a costume of virtue worn over ground nobody used for anything, and it spread from here across a continent. The same documents that mandated the grass also, quietly, barred Black families from buying in — the lawn and the conformity it advertised were always partly about who belonged. This is where the clearing instinct put on an apron and moved to the suburbs.

n: Marysville — maximum input, zero yield
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Marysville, Ohio — Scotts Miracle-Gro HQ)
det: Marysville, Ohio, US · present (Scotts HQ) — This is where all the cursed threads tie off: a corporate campus in Ohio that sells the chemicals, seed, and machinery for the strangest crop the most productive nation ever grew. The American lawn is the largest irrigated planting in the country — some three times the area of irrigated corn — and it yields nothing you can eat. Onto it go water, and synthetic nitrogen conjured at places like Oppau, and gasoline burned by the tankful, all to produce a uniform green surface most of whose owners never walk on. A hundred and nineteen billion dollars a year, worldwide, to maintain the appearance of control over living ground; three hundred and thirty billion for the wider trade of tidying nature. Maximum input, zero yield: the exact inverse of every closed-loop garden on the sacred list, which asks almost nothing and feeds people. The lawn takes everything — water, nitrogen, fuel, labour, the runoff running downstream to the dead zone already mapped — and hands back a costume. The clearing instinct, monetised, sold by the bag, and mowed every Saturday across a continent.

n: Bengal — the famine that proved hunger is not a lack of food
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Bengal delta, eastern India / Bangladesh; pin at Kolkata)
det: Bengal, India/Bangladesh (pin Kolkata) · 1943 — Between one and three million people died in Bengal in 1943, and the reason matters more than almost any single fact in this atlas: the rice had not run out. Grain sat in the region, moved through it, and left it on ships, while the people who grew it lost the wages and the prices that had been their only claim on it. Amartya Sen turned the lesson into a theorem - famine is hardly ever the world running short of food; it is people running short of the means to demand it. Bengal is the definition the rest of these pages are written against. The harvest was fine. What failed was the thread joining a hungry hand to a full sack.

n: Liverpool — the port where a nation first ate off someone else's ground
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Merseyside, NW England)
det: Merseyside, England · c. 1870–1914 — The idea that a country's food need not touch its own ground was invented, and this is one of the ports where it came ashore. From about 1870 the mills and cities of industrial Britain were fed on wheat and beef raised an ocean away - on the Plains of America, the Pampas of Argentina, the paddocks of Australia - land only just emptied of the people who had lived on it. The nutrients rode the new steamships one way: frontier to metropole, and never a return trip. British workers ate because someone else's continent had been cleared, and the soil that grew the bread never got the bodies back.

n: Food for Peace — the surplus that fed hunger and manufactured dependence
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Washington, D.C., USA)
det: Washington, D.C., US · PL-480, 1954–1973 — After the war America grew far more grain than it could eat - the gift of synthetic nitrogen and the new high-yield seed - and turned the mountain of surplus into policy. Subsidised grain shipped abroad as 'Food for Peace' fed real hunger and, in the same motion, undersold the farmers of the countries it fed, tilting whole nations toward imported wheat and Cold-War loyalty. It averted famines and hollowed out the self-sufficiency that might have prevented the next ones - building exactly the dependence the era after it would learn to charge for. The aid and the weapon travelled in one hold.

n: Bentonville — where the shelf, not the state, decides who eats
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Bentonville, Arkansas, USA)
det: Bentonville, Arkansas, US · 1980s–present — In the order the world runs on now, neither kings nor governments decide who eats - the retail buyer and the commodity trader do. A handful of firms move most of the world's traded grain, and the largest grocer on Earth sets both the price a farmer receives and the price a shopper pays; between them, food is routed to where the money is, not where the hunger is. This is not a flaw in the system. It is the system: allocate by effective demand, and call the resulting hunger a market outcome. Bentonville is the address at which Bengal became the permanent, planetary default.

n: The Chicago pit — the last mile that was never profitable to travel
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Chicago, Illinois, USA)
ctx: Chicago, Illinois, US · CBOT since 1848; present
det: Chicago, Illinois, US · CBOT since 1848; present — A bushel of wheat becomes a number here long before it becomes bread, and the number is what travels - bid, sold, hedged and shipped toward whoever can pay - while the last few miles to a hungry mouth go untravelled because no one could bill for them. Follow the arithmetic and the severance appears three times over: about a third of all the food the world grows is never eaten, households throwing out more than a billion meals a day; some six hundred and seventy million people are hungry anyway; and beneath a share of the abundance, tens of millions work under force to bring it in. None of these is a shortage. Each is a claim that failed to connect - a loop cut, and priced as if it were the weather.

n: The Great Oxidation — the first world life poisoned was its own
host: TBD — cartographer/research to assign (Huronian Supergroup / Gowganda Fm, north of Lake Huron, Ontario; event was global)
ctx: planet-wide (rocks: Huronian, Ontario) · ~2.4 billion years ago
det: planet-wide (rocks: Huronian, Ontario) · ~2.4 billion years ago — Long before anyone could be blamed for it, life did to the planet what this whole atlas is about. A microbe in the Archean sea learned to eat sunlight and water and breathed out oxygen as waste. When the oceans and rocks could soak up no more, the gas turned on the world that made it - poison to almost everything alive, corroding the anaerobic biosphere into the muds and deep water where its heirs still hide. Worse, it burned away the methane that had kept the young, faint-sunned Earth warm, and the planet froze nearly to the equator. The first great pollution, the first mass die-back, and the first climate catastrophe were one event - and the culprit was life itself, pumping out, with no way to stop, the very gas we now cannot live without.
